title: #devex: 1767685544
preview:
Ibrahim Khan: Kicking off a quick review of retryable status codes + network error classification across SDKs. We’ve got drift between Python (requests/httpx), TS (fetch), Go (net/http). Want to lock a shared table today so conformance tests can encode it.

Ibrahim Khan: Current proposal (non-streaming): retry on 408, 429, 500, 502, 503, 504 + network/transport errors. Do NOT retry on 400/401/403/404. Open question: 409/423/425? and what we consider a “network error” in each lang.

Sabrina James: +1. In python we currently have a mix:
- requests: `requests.exceptions.Timeout` and `ConnectionError` (includes DNS, refused, reset)
- httpx: `httpx.TimeoutException` vs `httpx.RequestError` (super broad)
Right now we accidentally retry some httpx RequestError cases that are actually local config (bad proxy) and it’s noisy.

Dana Lo: In TS it’s messy because `fetch` throws a `TypeError` for *any* network failure (DNS, refused, CORS-ish, etc). Abort is `AbortError` (DOMException) depending on runtime.
We should treat Abort as NOT retryable (caller canceled / timeout) unless it’s our own internal per-attempt timeout?

Mateusz Zielinski: Go: net/http returns `context deadline exceeded` for timeouts, and `net.Error` w/ Timeout() = true. Also you can get errors when reading the body (EOF, unexpected EOF). Some of those happen after headers were received.
We need to decide whether “read error mid-body” is retryable (esp. if request might have been processed).

Allison Grant: From SRE side, keep retry surface tight. Status codes: 408/429/5xx as listed is good. Please no retries on 409 by default—too many APIs use it as a real conflict.
If platform ever emits a specific error_code for transient lock/overload, we can key off that instead.

Ibrahim Khan: 👍 ok, so baseline codes: [408, 429, 500, 502, 503, 504].
Question: should we include 520/522 etc (CDN-ish) if they appear? We mostly front w/ our gateway; sometimes customers hit a proxy.

Ethan Park: Gateway shouldn’t emit 520/522, but proxies/CDNs might. I’d rather not encode those unless we see them in support volume. If we do, we should treat unknown 5xx as retryable already.

Sabrina James: “unknown 5xx” => if status >=500 and <=599, retryable except maybe 501? But 501 is “not implemented” and is likely terminal. Still rare.

Dana Lo: Agree. In TS we can do `if (status >= 500 && status !== 501)` as retryable. But to keep parity, we should document exact list vs range?

Allison Grant: Prefer range for 5xx (except 501) so we don’t miss 507/508 etc. But keep the docs explicit about what the SDK does.

Ibrahim Khan: Ok: retryable status predicate:
- status == 408
- status == 429
- status in [500..599] AND status != 501
Anything else => not retry.

Mateusz Zielinski: Works for Go.

Sabrina James: Works for Python.

Dana Lo: Works for TS.

Ibrahim Khan: Now network errors.
My take: retryable if request didn’t definitively reach server OR we didn’t get an HTTP response. That usually means: connect errors, TLS handshake errors, connection reset before headers, DNS, etc.
But if we got a response status, we use the status rules.

Sabrina James: For httpx: if we get `httpx.HTTPStatusError`, it’s a response (don’t treat as network). If we get `httpx.RequestError`, we didn’t get a response. That’s our “network error bucket” (retryable) BUT there are some non-transient cases like invalid URL.
We can filter obvious programmer errors: InvalidURL, UnsupportedProtocol -> not retry.

Dana Lo: JS: no response => fetch rejects. That’s our network bucket. But also CORS can cause fetch rejection in browsers; we don’t officially support browser use, but people do.
Maybe: treat fetch rejection as retryable *only* in Node runtimes? or just retry and let them disable.

Monica Patel: Let’s avoid runtime branching. Default behavior should be consistent. If browser folks complain, we can document “SDKs are server-side; in browsers you should manage retries yourself.”

Dana Lo: ok. For Abort:
- If AbortController triggered because user canceled: not retryable.
- If our internal attempt-timeout triggers an abort: that is retryable (it’s equivalent to per-attempt timeout).
But in practice both look like AbortError unless we tag the signal.

Ibrahim Khan: Suggestion: in TS, wrap attempt timeout abort in a custom error before it bubbles. i.e. map internal timeout to `TimeoutError` (retryable) and map user abort to `AbortError` (not retryable).

Dana Lo: Yep. We can create our own `RequestAbortedError` vs `RequestTimeoutError`.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go has similar: if ctx canceled by user, `context.Canceled` (not retryable). If ctx deadline exceeded due to our attempt timeout, it’s retryable. But we need to ensure we can differentiate “overall timeout reached” vs “attempt timeout hit”.

Allison Grant: Please be careful w/ retrying on overall timeouts. If overall timeout is reached, there should be no further attempts.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. Retry loop should stop on overall deadline.

Sabrina James: Python: same. If user sets overall timeout to 10s and we’ve already used 10s, we stop.

Ibrahim Khan: What about read errors / EOF mid-body?
Example: Go streaming or non-streaming where server started response but connection drops.

Mateusz Zielinski: For non-streaming: if we’re reading body and it fails before we parse JSON, we can treat as retryable *if* method is idempotent and no idempotency concerns. But our API calls aren’t strictly idempotent (billing, tokens). So default should be: retryable only if we have not received response headers.

Allison Grant: Strong preference: if headers received, do not auto-retry. It can duplicate completions.

Dana Lo: In TS fetch, you can get a response object then stream read fails. Same issue. So rule: network error before any response headers => retryable. After headers => surface error, no retry.

Sabrina James: That’s implementable. In httpx, RequestError means no response at all; if there’s a response but read fails you’ll get something else (or the stream iterator throws). We can treat streaming separately.

Ibrahim Khan: Great. So network error definition for retry loop:
- Retryable: connect/DNS/TLS/connection reset where NO HTTP response was obtained.
- Not retryable: user cancellation; errors after response headers; invalid URL/config errors.

Ibrahim Khan: Also: ensure we respect `Retry-After` on 429. If present, it overrides computed backoff (capped).

Allison Grant: cap + jitter please. And make sure parsing supports both `Retry-After: 5` and HTTP-date.

Dana Lo: We already parse both in TS; need to align max cap (we currently cap at 60s).

Sabrina James: Python PR in progress; will match cap at 60s too.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go currently ignores Retry-After (oops). Will add.

Sean Gallagher: Jumping in—this is good. One more SRE ask: ensure retries on 503 don’t hammer when gateway returns `Retry-After`. During incidents we’ll use that header to slow clients.

Ibrahim Khan: 👍 action items:
- I’ll update parity matrix row: retryable status predicate (408,429,5xx!=501), network error definition (no response headers), and “no retry after headers received.”
- Dana: TS mapping for internal timeout abort vs user abort.
- Sabrina: python filter InvalidURL/UnsupportedProtocol as non-retry.
- Mateusz: Go classify ctx.Canceled vs deadline exceeded + ensure no retry after headers.

Monica Patel: Please make sure this lands in conformance fixtures with a few canonical cases:
1) 500 -> retries
2) 501 -> no retry
3) fetch reject (no response) -> retries
4) streaming read disconnect after headers -> no auto retry

Ibrahim Khan: Will do. I’ll drop a short snippet in the conformance plan doc + link here when ready.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: once you update, tag me and I’ll sanity check for retry storm risk.

Dana Lo: :+1: will push TS changes behind existing retry middleware so it’s not a breaking API.

Sabrina James: 👍

Mateusz Zielinski: 👍

preview:
Aisha Khan: Quick kickoff - we need an enablement schedule for the upcoming onboarding wave (Jun-Jul). Proposal: two weekly office hours + one weekly technical deep-dive. Looking for owners and a lean runbook.
Diego Morales: I can run Monday 15:00 GMT drop-in (EMEA-friendly). Will keep it light, 45m.
Ruth Lin: I can take the Wednesday APAC slot at 10:00 HKT for first 4 weeks.
Ethan Park: I'll cover the technical deep-dive on Fridays 11:00 PT. Focus on migration playbooks and Dedicated -> Private nuances.
Aisha Khan: Nice, thanks. On format: 30m guided demo + 20m open triage for drop-ins. Deep-dive: 60m with pre-collected tickets. Anyone opposed to requiring an RSVP for the deep-dive to prioritize topics?
Diego Morales: RSVP makes sense for deep-dive. Also recommend capture of "customer objective" in RSVP so we can tailor examples.
Ruth Lin: Agreed. Can we add a short question: "Primary environment" (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) + "Main blocker"?
OnboardingBot: RSVP form generated: https://forms.redwood.example/enablement-deepdive. Auto-attach agenda template to accepted responses.
Aisha Khan: Great. Draft runbook snippet: 
```
Enablement runbook v0.1:
- 5m: welcome + account context
- 25/30m: demo / guided walkthrough
- 20/30m: live troubleshooting + triage
- 5m: recap, owners, next steps
Post-session: create thread notes, tag account owner, create Jira if bug/work needed
```
Ethan Park: For deep-dive, please add pre-read: migration checklist + sample logs. I'll post the deck by Wednesday.
Diego Morales: Small ask: can CS ops generate a list of accounts with renewals in next 90 days to prioritize invites?
Ruth Lin: That list would help me prioritize APAC time slots too.
Aisha Khan: Noted - @Ethan @Diego @Ruth I'll loop in CS ops. If any blockers, post here by Thursday EOD. Also volunteers: rotate backup leads in case of conflicts? Suggestions: pair leads for each slot.
Ethan Park: I'll be backup for Monday/EMEA if Diego can't make it.
Diego Morales: Thanks. I'll add the recurring invites and put the calendar links here once done.
OnboardingBot: Calendar events created: "Enablement Drop-in (Mon GMT)", "Enablement Drop-in (Wed HKT)", "Technical Deep-dive (Fri PT)". Public RSVP: https://forms.redwood.example/enablement-deepdive.
Aisha Khan: Perfect. I'll add a short feedback pulse post-session (1-question NPS + 1-line resource ask) and share the form.
Ruth Lin: Love it. That will help our continuous improvement loop.
Diego Morales: All good from my side. Recurring invites accepted.
Ethan Park: Accepted. See you Friday for the first deep-dive.
Aisha Khan: Thanks team. Posting runbook to CS playbook now and tagging ops. Reply with blockers or suggestions before EOD Thursday. :rocket:

title: #architecture: 1763894410
preview:
Noah Patel: Dropping ADR draft here for final nitpicks before I mark “in review” -> “accepted”: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/ADR-override-order

Noah Patel: TL;DR: how we ship the hardware tuning profile pack + override precedence (fleet defaults vs per-pool vs per-deploy vs per-route/customer), plus rollback semantics. Would love eyes from Infra/SRE + anyone who’s been burned by config drift.

Ethan Park: skimming now. first nit: the precedence list in section 3 reads like “later wins” but then section 4 implies “fleet defaults override customer overrides for safety”. those are contradictory.

Ethan Park: can we explicitly state: (a) which layers are allowed to override which keys (key-level allowlist), and (b) whether safety clamps are applied after merge. otherwise readers will assume you can override anything via env var.

Benji Okafor: +1. I also think “env vars highest precedence” is too blunt. We’ve been using env as emergency toggle, but in Dedicated/Private it becomes a footgun (random helm chart sets env, overrides everything silently).

Benji Okafor: Suggest: env vars only for a small set of debug/kill-switch knobs (e.g. DISABLE_PROFILE_PACK, FORCE_PROFILE_ID), not arbitrary scheduler settings.

Diego Santos: from deployment compatibility angle: Hosted will use control-plane pushed config, Dedicated/Private via helm values + config file mount. if we keep “env always wins”, we’re creating different behavior across modes b/c not all modes have env injection in the same place.

Diego Santos: we should define override sources in a way that maps cleanly:
- runtime baked defaults
- packaged profile pack
- external profile bundle (optional)
- deployment config (control plane / helm values)
- request-level overrides (route/customer)
- emergency toggles
and then specify priority.

Noah Patel: good call. I was trying to keep it simple but yeah, it reads inconsistent.

Quentin Harris: another nit: the ADR says “profile version pinned per deployment” but doesn’t say what happens if runtime doesn’t support that profile version.

Quentin Harris: we need to specify compatibility check behavior: fail hard vs warn + fallback. for Private, fail hard might brick a cluster during upgrade; for Hosted, failing hard is scary too.

Neha Kapoor: +1 please don’t “fail hard” by default. SRE preference: startup should be conservative:
- if explicit override requested (FORCE_PROFILE_ID/profile_version pinned) and incompatible -> fail fast (user asked for it)
- if auto-selected profile incompatible -> log loudly + fall back to safe baseline profile (or runtime defaults)

Neha Kapoor: also want rollback semantics called out: “disable profile pack for pool X” should be reversible without redeploy (i.e. control plane flag / config map change). ADR currently reads like “ship inside runtime binary” which makes rollback = new runtime deploy.

Irene Choi: infra side: we can do both, but we have to say what’s default. baked-in pack is good for Private/offline installs, but Hosted needs a way to hotfix profiles without waiting for runtime release train.

Irene Choi: maybe state: “runtime includes last-known-good pack; control plane can optionally mount/serve newer pack; selection chooses external pack if signed + compatible.”

Connor O'Brien: release train concern: if ADR encourages “external bundle can override baked pack”, we need a clear ownership + signoff gate. otherwise teams will bypass runtime release process.

Connor O'Brien: ask: who is allowed to publish external pack versions? Runtime kernels/scheduling only? and do we require perf-canary green on all SKUs before promotion?

Paula Mendes: from canary/rollback requirements: please add explicit “blast radius” notes. e.g.
- default path is “enable new pack in canary pool only”
- rollback trigger thresholds (p95, error rate, OOM) should flip DISABLE_PROFILE_PACK at pool scope
- per-customer override should never be the first rollback lever during incident (too slow)

Marissa Cole: product/UX nit: ADR says “per-route override wins” but we should be careful: customers will expect per-route config to be stable even if fleet defaults change.

Marissa Cole: can we state “customer-visible overrides are sticky + audited” and that we’ll expose “effective profile + source” in console? otherwise Support is going to get murdered.

Benji Okafor: config drift risk: ADR should explicitly mention “normalized defaults” problem.

Benji Okafor: right now we have runtime hard-coded defaults + profile pack defaults. if both exist, people change one and forget the other -> drift. proposal: runtime hard-coded defaults are minimal safe baseline; all tuning lives in pack; runtime logs effective config at boot.

Ethan Park: yes pls. ADR should require:
1) single source of truth for default values (pack)
2) runtime prints “effective config” (or at least key diffs) + profile id/version
3) validation rejects unknown keys (to prevent silent no-ops)

Diego Santos: also “override order” should be deterministic and observable. i.e. metrics tag: selected_profile_id, selected_profile_version, override_source (auto/bundle/control-plane/helm/env).

Neha Kapoor: +1000 on observability. and please call out that we need an oncall-friendly toggle:
- disable profile selection entirely (fallback to baseline)
- disable only external bundle, keep baked-in
- force baseline profile (per pool)

Noah Patel: ok, proposed edits based on this thread:
- rewrite precedence section as an ordered list with “who can override what” (key allowlist + safety clamps applied last)
- split “env vars” into “emergency toggles only”
- add compatibility behavior matrix (explicit override vs auto-select)
- clarify distribution: baked-in LKG pack + optional externally served pack with signoff
- add config drift mitigation requirements: single source of truth + startup logs + effective-config dump + metrics

Noah Patel: I’ll push a revision today and tag you all. Anything else blocking acceptance?

Quentin Harris: one more nit: “per-customer override” should be clearly separated from “per-route override” in ADR language. per-customer might apply across multiple routes + models; per-route is more granular. readers will conflate.

Irene Choi: +1. also mention GPU SKU detection variability (string variants) -> if detection ambiguous, we should prefer baseline rather than guessing and picking wrong profile.

Paula Mendes: not blocking, but please include “roll forward vs rollback” guidance: if pack is bad, disable quickly; if only one knob is bad, prefer hotfix in external bundle + canary before re-enabling.

Connor O'Brien: as long as external bundle path has a clear promotion gate + ownership, I’m good.

Neha Kapoor: same. make sure ADR doesn’t sound like “expected behavior == guarantee” re perf. :thumbsup:

Noah Patel: noted. will update wording to “expected behavior / safe-by-default” and tie guarantees to SLO docs not ADR.

Marissa Cole: +1. also please add a note that console will show “effective profile” but doesn’t promise a fixed latency number.

Noah Patel: cool. pushing update. thanks all.

- request-level overrides (route/customer)
- emergency toggles
and then specify priority.

Noah Patel: good call. I was trying to keep it simple but yeah, it reads inconsistent.

Quentin Harris: another nit: the ADR says “profile version pinned per deployment” but doesn’t say what happens if runtime doesn’t support that profile version.

Quentin Harris: we need to specify compatibility check behavior: fail hard vs warn + fallback. for Private, fail hard might brick a cluster during upgrade; for Hosted, failing hard is scary too.

Neha Kapoor: +1 please don’t “fail hard” by default. SRE preference: startup should be conservative:
- if explicit override requested (FORCE_PROFILE_ID/profile_version pinned) and incompatible -> fail fast (user asked for it)
- if auto-selected profile incompatible -> log loudly + fall back to safe baseline profile (or runtime defaults)

Neha Kapoor: also want rollback semantics called out: “disable profile pack for pool X” should be reversible without redeploy (i.e. control plane flag / config map change). ADR currently reads like “ship inside runtime binary” which makes rollback = new runtime deploy.

Irene Choi: infra side: we can do both, but we have to say what’s default. baked-in pack is good for Private/offline installs, but Hosted needs a way to hotfix profiles without waiting for runtime release train.

Irene Choi: maybe state: “runtime includes last-known-good pack; control plane can optionally mount/serve newer pack; selection chooses external pack if signed + compatible.”

Connor O'Brien: release train concern: if ADR encourages “external bundle can override baked pack”, we need a clear ownership + signoff gate. otherwise teams will bypass runtime release process.

Connor O'Brien: ask: who is allowed to publish external pack versions? Runtime kernels/scheduling only? and do we require perf-canary green on all SKUs before promotion?

Paula Mendes: from canary/rollback requirements: please add explicit “blast radius” notes. e.g.
- default path is “enable new pack in canary pool only”
- rollback trigger thresholds (p95, error rate, OOM) should flip DISABLE_PROFILE_PACK at pool scope
- per-customer override should never be the first rollback lever during incident (too slow)

Marissa Cole: product/UX nit: ADR says “per-route override wins” but we should be careful: customers will expect per-route config to be stable even if fleet defaults change.

Marissa Cole: can we state “customer-visible overrides are sticky + audited” and that we’ll expose “effective profile + source” in console? otherwise Support is going to get murdered.

Benji Okafor: config drift risk: ADR should explicitly mention “normalized defaults” problem.

Benji Okafor: right now we have runtime hard-coded defaults + profile pack defaults. if both exist, people change one and forget the other -> drift. proposal: runtime hard-coded defaults are minimal safe baseline; all tuning lives in pack; runtime logs effective config at boot.

Ethan Park: yes pls. ADR should require:
1) single source of truth for default values (pack)
2) runtime prints “effective config” (or at least key diffs) + profile id/version
3) validation rejects unknown keys (to prevent silent no-ops)

Diego Santos: also “override order” should be deterministic and observable. i.e. metrics tag: selected_profile_id, selected_profile_version, override_source (auto/bundle/control-plane/helm/env).

Neha Kapoor: +1000 on observability. and please call out that we need an oncall-friendly toggle:
- disable profile selection entirely (fallback to baseline)
- disable only external bundle, keep baked-in
- force baseline profile (per pool)

Noah Patel: ok, proposed edits based on this thread:
- rewrite precedence section as an ordered list with “who can override what” (key allowlist + safety clamps applied last)
- split “env vars” into “emergency toggles only”
- add compatibility behavior matrix (explicit override vs auto-select)
- clarify distribution: baked-in LKG pack + optional externally served pack with signoff
- add config drift mitigation requirements: single source of truth + startup logs + effective-config dump + metrics

Noah Patel: I’ll push a revision today and tag you all. Anything else blocking acceptance?

Quentin Harris: one more nit: “per-customer override” should be clearly separated from “per-route override” in ADR language. per-customer might apply across multiple routes + models; per-route is more granular. readers will conflate.

Irene Choi: +1. also mention GPU SKU detection variability (string variants) -> if detection ambiguous, we should prefer baseline rather than guessing and picking wrong profile.

Paula Mendes: not blocking, but please include “roll forward vs rollback” guidance: if pack is bad, disable quickly; if only one knob is bad, prefer hotfix in external bundle + canary before re-enabling.

Connor O'Brien: as long as external bundle path has a clear promotion gate + ownership, I’m good.

Neha Kapoor: same. make sure ADR doesn’t sound like “expected behavior == guarantee” re perf. :thumbsup:

Noah Patel: noted. will update wording to “expected behavior / safe-by-default” and tie guarantees to SLO docs not ADR.

Marissa Cole: +1. also please add a note that console will show “effective profile” but doesn’t promise a fixed latency number.

Noah Patel: cool. pushing update. thanks all.

title: #eng-sre: 1763611907
preview:
Rafael Mendes: post-launch metrics check for hot-route protection (GA enablement window ended ~2h ago). Can folks sanity check: tail latency + 5xx for protected routes vs last week? any weirdness w/ shed/429 reason codes?

Sergio Costa: looking at the "Hot Route Protection - Enterprise" dashboard (us-east + eu-west). Early read is good.
- /v1/chat/completions enterprise p99: 3.9s -> 2.6s (24h rolling)
- /v1/embeddings enterprise p99: 1.3s -> 0.9s
- 5xx rate: down ~45% on protected routes (mostly timeouts)
Annotations line up w/ flag enablement.

Aisha Bello: +1. Error budget burn improved too.
- Chat enterprise SLO (99.9%): burn rate was ~2.1x during peaks last week; now hovering ~0.7x since rollout.
- Best-effort getting throttled earlier (expected), but seeing fewer fleet-wide latency spikes.
I’m not seeing any paging storms from the new alerts either (nice).

incident-bot: Overload protection activations (last 60m):
- region=us-east route_group=chat tier=best_effort action=admission_control events=7 max_shed_rate=18% duration_max=6m dashboard=https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/hot-route-protect
- region=eu-west route_group=embeddings tier=best_effort action=quota_gate events=2 max_429_rps=220 duration_max=2m
Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SRE/Runbook+Overload+Protection+and+Admission+Control

Logan Wright: telemetry view: overload_reason_code breakdown looks clean (no new high-cardinality offenders).
Top 429 reasons since GA:
- best-effort: OVERLOAD_ADMISSION (62%), QUOTA_EXCEEDED (34%), POLICY_DENY (4%)
- enterprise: QUOTA_EXCEEDED (88%), OVERLOAD_ADMISSION (8%), ROUTE_BUDGET (4%)
So enterprise is mostly quota, not overload shedding.

Rafael Mendes: love to see enterprise not getting punted. For best-effort 18% shed rate spikes — does that correlate w/ GPU saturation?

Vanessa Ortiz: checked fleet signals (us-east). Those 6m windows correspond to GPU util ~92-95% + queue depth crossing the trigger threshold. Autoscaler was already adding nodes but warmup lag meant the routing gate kicked in first.
Net effect: enterprise queue depth stays flat, best-effort queue depth stops growing (no cascade).

Ethan Park: gateway side looks stable. No increase in gateway CPU, policy eval cache hit rate ~97%.
We did see a small uptick in 429s overall (+12% vs baseline) but that’s concentrated in best-effort and matches the new behavior.

Aisha Bello: any reports from Support yet on “why am I getting more 429s”? I expect some, but want to make sure messaging points to retry/backoff + reason code headers.

Logan Wright: headers are present in samples I pulled:
`x-redwood-throttle-reason: overload_admission`
`retry-after: 1`
Also seeing the structured body in most clients. There are a few missing `retry-after` (looks like old edge pods?) — might be a rollout straggler.

Sergio Costa: I’ll add a panel to track “429 missing retry-after header” rate. If it’s >0.5% we should chase it.

Rafael Mendes: action items / follow-ups:
1) Create ticket to investigate missing `retry-after` on some 429s (assign gateway). We should not make Support guess.
2) Tune eu-west embeddings threshold slightly? We’re seeing quota_gate events; might be config mismatch or just traffic burst.
3) Add a short internal note for on-call: what shed % is “expected” before we page (so folks don’t panic).

Ethan Park: I’ll open (1) as ENG follow-up. Suspect some pods still on legacy throttle middleware. We can confirm by build hash.

Aisha Bello: for (3), I can PR the runbook w/ a “normal vs abnormal” section. E.g. best-effort shed up to 20% for <10m w/ stable enterprise p99 is OK; page if enterprise shed >1% sustained 5m or enterprise p99 burn >2x for 10m.

Vanessa Ortiz: on (2) — eu-west had a capacity dip earlier (one GPU pool drained for maintenance). If we’re stable now, I’d prefer we don’t loosen thresholds until we have a week of data. Maybe we just add an annotation for that maintenance window.

Sergio Costa: +1. Also seeing enterprise p95 stable in eu-west, so protections did what we wanted.

Rafael Mendes: cool. Can someone link the post-launch review ticket? want everything captured.

Aisha Bello: ENG-4931 is the post-launch metrics review + followups. I’ll comment with today’s deltas + these actions.

Logan Wright: I’ll also drop a note re: cardinality staying flat + the minor `retry-after` gap.

Rafael Mendes: thanks. If no fires by EOD, we can call this “healthy” and shift to the cleanup items. :thumbsup:

title: #architecture: 1762231191
preview:
Ravi Menon: dropping the WIP ADR/design link here for review (enforcement points + policy eval contract). would love eyes esp on trade-offs + auditability: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/hot-route-capacity-protection-adr-wip

Ravi Menon: key question: where do we enforce tier-aware protection? API gateway vs routing service vs runtime scheduler (or layered). constraints: low latency overhead, deterministic/explainable decisions for enterprise escalations, no new cascading failure mode.

Ethan Park: from control plane view, I’m leaning layered:
- gateway: static-ish rate limits/quotas + consistent 429 schema
- routing: admission control when we have fleet pressure signals (queue depth / gpu saturation)
- runtime: last-ditch backpressure to avoid total collapse
biggest risk is adding per-request policy network hop.

Vikram Singh: +1 on layered, but we need to be super clear what executes on the hot path. if gateway calls policy-engine over RPC per request, we’re dead.
what’s the expected overhead target? i’d say <= ~1ms p50 added, and importantly no p99 blowups under pressure.

Jordan Lee: policy eval plan in the draft is “local eval w/ cached policy snapshot”, not synchronous RPC.
- control plane publishes policy bundles (signed) to gateway + routing
- deterministic evaluation in-process
- decision log emitted async (sampling configurable)

auditability: we can guarantee “same inputs => same output” via versioned bundles + stable rules.

Amara Diallo: from gateway side: yes please keep it in-process. we can load the bundle into the existing limiter module.
also we need consistent 429 reasons:
- quota exceeded (window)
- rate limit exceeded (burst)
- overload shedding (admission)
- upstream backpressure (runtime)
if we mix 429/503 without clear semantics, support is going to hate us.

Allison Grant: on metrics/tags: do NOT tag by customer_id in the primary counters. we already got burned w cardinality.
route_group + tier + decision_reason + region should be enough for dashboards.
for audit, log the customer/tenant details in a separate sampled structured log stream (or on-demand query) rather than in metrics.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also: tier attribution can’t come from client headers. needs to be derived from auth context (API key -> tenant -> contract tier) and then passed through as signed internal context.
if routing is doing admission based on tier, make sure it can’t be spoofed.

Peter Holtz: concern w “routing does admission”: when routing is degraded (or policy bundle stale), do we fail open or fail closed?
for enterprise SLO protection, i’d rather fail closed for best-effort and fail open for enterprise (i.e., preserve enterprise capacity even if policy eval is busted).
need explicit fallback behavior in ADR.

Mina Haddad: routing angle:
- we can gate best-effort first once queue depth crosses threshold
- we can also redirect best-effort to alternate pools/regions (when allowed)
- enterprise routes: prefer strict admission vs silent fallback unless product signs off (quality changes)
question: do we allow model fallback for /v1/chat/completions enterprise? or only for embeddings/rerank?

Rafael Mendes: SRE asks:
1) how do we debug “why was I 429’d” on-call? need a clear reason code + linkable decision context.
2) if admission control flips on due to pressure, we need an event + annotation on dashboards.
3) runbook should say: check queue depth, shed rate by tier, and which component is shedding (gateway vs routing vs runtime).

Sofia Ivanova: runtime perspective: runtime backpressure is necessary but we should avoid making it the primary enforcement. by the time runtime sees it, latency is already bad.
we can export scheduler_lag + queue_depth and also expose a “reject new work” signal upstream, but that needs to be coarse to avoid thrash.

Vikram Singh: +1. runtime shedding should be the circuit breaker, not the policy engine.
also: please define “capacity signal contract” (names, update cadence, smoothing). if routing is reading a noisy signal, we’ll oscillate and create weird tail latency.

Jordan Lee: auditability details:
- every decision includes: policy_bundle_version, route_group_id, tier, decision (allow/throttle/shed), reason_code
- include a “decision_id” hash over inputs so support can reference it
- store full input tuple in sampled logs only (request_id, tenant_id, route, model, limits)

Amara Diallo: for headers in 429, proposal:
- `x-redwood-retry-after-ms`
- `x-redwood-limit-reason` (enum)
- `x-redwood-policy-version`
body includes same fields + human copy.
need to confirm if we can return retry-after for overload shedding (sometimes answer is “don’t retry immediately”).

Allison Grant: pls avoid per-request “retry-after” that changes wildly; we should bucket/smooth it. otherwise clients retry in lockstep.
maybe return `retry-after` only for quota/rate limits; for overload return a fixed backoff suggestion.

Peter Holtz: on fail open/closed: i want crisp:
- if policy bundle missing/invalid: gateway defaults to existing global limits (safe), routing defaults to “protect enterprise” (shed best-effort earlier)
- if capacity signals missing: routing should degrade to static per-tier budgets, no fancy shaping.

Benji Okafor: rollout/ops notes:
- feature flag at gateway + routing separately
- allowlist enterprise tenants first
- we need rollback switch that *immediately* disables routing admission (since that’s most likely to surprise customers)
- policy bundle version should be pinned per region so we can roll forward/back.

Mina Haddad: question on latency overhead: if gateway does quota + rate check + policy eval, what’s the per-request CPU cost? we should benchmark.
Vikram can we get an agreed budget? (i’ll add to design acceptance criteria)

Vikram Singh: budget suggestion:
- added gateway overhead: p50 < 0.5ms, p99 < 2ms
- routing admission check: should be constant-time and in-process (no extra store read per request)
anything requiring redis roundtrips is out.

Ravi Menon: this is super helpful. i’ll update ADR with:
- layered enforcement (gateway quotas/rate + routing admission + runtime circuit breaker)
- explicit fail open/closed behavior (protect enterprise by default)
- audit log vs metrics cardinality split
- latency budgets + “no synchronous RPC” rule

Rafael Mendes: can we also add an internal debug endpoint spec somewhere? on-call will need a way to ask “given tenant X + route Y, what would policy do?” without tailing logs for an hour.

Jordan Lee: we have a draft for a restricted “policy explain” endpoint (admin-only). will add to ADR as follow-up item; needs authz + audit trail.

Amara Diallo: +1. support asks for “explain 429” all the time. even a console-only tool would help.

Allison Grant: make sure any explain endpoint is rate limited + audited; we don’t want it to become another hot path.

Ravi Menon: action items i’m capturing:
- (Ravi) update ADR w the above + post in #architecture for final sign-off
- (Jordan) add concrete audit fields + sampling guidance
- (Amara) propose final 429 reason codes + headers
- (Allison/Rafael) clarify metrics vs logs + dashboard annotations

Ravi Menon: will repost revised draft tomorrow AM.

title: #customer-success: 1762145790
preview:
Maya Chen: Heads-up — Aurelia Health renewal in ~45 days. We're seeing a ~60% drop in weekly API calls since Monday. Main technical POC (former CTO) left two weeks ago. AE mentioned a competitor trial. Need quick triage and an outreach plan. :eyes:
Jordan Lee: Confirmed. Had a 1:1 with their AE — procurement is talking to Nimbus AI for a narrow use-case. Not full churn signal but seat activity is down, esp on the `reporting` endpoint. Dashboard: https://redwood.app/d/abc123/aurelia-usage
Ethan Park: Can you paste the query you used? I'll pull token-level metrics and error logs for the last 14 days.
Maya Chen: SQL I ran:```sql
SELECT date, SUM(api_calls) AS calls, SUM(errors) AS errs
FROM usage_events
WHERE customer = 'aurelia_health'
GROUP BY date
ORDER BY date DESC
LIMIT 14;
```
Ethan Park: Pulled logs. Notes: generation calls down 63%, embeddings steady. Error rate rose 0.2% -> 1.5%, mostly timeouts/429s from us-east-1. A lot of retries showing fallbacks to smaller model variants. Could be routing congestion + their traffic pattern changed.
Priya Raman: If embeddings are stable but gen is down, could be front-end toggles or a product change. Playbook suggestion: immediate CSM check-in + offer a 1-week dedicated burst to validate latency (no big discount, just reserved capacity). Also recommend Solutions Eng do a shadow session.

title: #eng-infra: 1760710033
preview:
Sahana Iyer: heads up: the signature verification integration tests are now the slowest step in `private-installer` CI 😬

Sahana Iyer: `bundle-verify-integration` job went from ~6m to ~28-35m after we added tamper/replay/downgrade cases. We need to get this back under ~10m or we’re gonna start timing out in release train.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118244 for redwood-private-installer / main failed
ci-bot: Step `bundle-verify-integration` exceeded soft timeout (30m). Log: https://buildkite.example.redwood/...

Devon Price: looking at the logs now. a lot of time is `sha256sum` over big fixtures + re-generating offline bundle each test case.

Ethan Park: yeah, each test currently:
1) builds bundle (tar + generate manifest)
2) signs manifest
3) unpacks
4) modifies artifact
5) runs preflight

Ethan Park: so we’re doing the expensive steps N times.

Jordan Lee: manifest canonicalization step is also re-running every time. not huge but it adds up.

Sahana Iyer: can we make a “golden” bundle fixture once per job and then derive tampered variants cheaply?

Devon Price: +1. also the fixture includes a big image tarball (we added it to simulate offline image import). hashing that is killing us.

Connor O'Brien: release side: we don’t actually need a 3GB image tar to validate signature semantics. we need to validate that we *would* hash the referenced artifact, but the artifact can be small in tests.

Marcus Lin: from security POV, as long as the test still exercises:
- content hash validation
- signature verification
- fail-closed behavior
- downgrade protection
we can shrink the bytes.

Sean Gallagher: also worth splitting “fast unit-ish verification tests” vs “true end-to-end on a full bundle”. run the full bundle one nightly?

Ethan Park: we already have unit tests for canonicalization + envelope parsing. the integration suite is meant to be “bundle on disk + preflight CLI”.

Ethan Park: but we could do:
- one e2e uses a “medium” bundle (still realistic layout)
- tamper variants derived from that bundle via file copy + single edit

Sahana Iyer: what’s the slowest operation by flame graph / rough breakdown?

Devon Price: rough from build logs:
- `create_bundle()` ~9-12m (mostly tar + sha256s)
- `verify_bundle()` ~30-60s each
- repeated 12 times = pain

Devon Price: also `docker save` in one fixture is ~4m. why are we doing docker save here?

Ethan Park: legacy from older offline install tests. for signing, we only need to test that the manifest lists artifacts + hashes and we verify them offline.

Jordan Lee: can we replace docker save fixture with just a dummy file named `images/redwood-runtime.tar` that is small?

Marcus Lin: yes, but keep the path + semantics. the test should ensure that *changing that file* triggers hash mismatch.

Sahana Iyer: ok. proposal:
1) replace heavy image fixture with small deterministic tar (like `tar --mtime=0 --sort=name ...`)
2) generate base bundle once, cache it across test cases
3) for each test: copy base bundle dir (hardlink?) and mutate only one file

Sahana Iyer: for (2) we can do `TestMain` / shared setup if in Go.

Ethan Park: suite is in Go. we can use `sync.Once` and write base bundle into `$TMPDIR/base_bundle`.

Devon Price: copying the bundle dir might still be expensive. can we do copy-on-write? linux hardlinks via `cp -al`?

Sahana Iyer: Buildkite agents are linux, so `cp -al` should work. but careful: mutating file should break hardlink (need to copy the file you mutate, not write in-place).

Jordan Lee: for tamper cases we usually modify manifest or one artifact. we can do:
- copy tree as hardlinks
- for the file to modify, `cp` it first to break link, then edit

Ethan Park: makes sense.

Connor O'Brien: also add `BUILDKITE_CACHE`/artifact caching? we could upload the base bundle as build artifact for the step and have other steps consume it.

Sahana Iyer: within a single step we can probably avoid cross-step caching. cross-build caching is tricky (branch isolation) but possible.

Devon Price: simplest win is: stop regenerating bundle per test. right now it’s literally in each test function.

Ethan Park: i can refactor to build once.

Sean Gallagher: do we have `-run TestBundleVerifyTamper.*` set to run serial? go tests default parallel at package level but not within suite.

Devon Price: we turned off `t.Parallel()` previously because tests were stomping on temp dirs.

Sahana Iyer: if we make temp dirs unique per test (and base bundle read-only), we can turn `t.Parallel()` back on for mutation/verify steps.

Ethan Park: verify step reads from disk + computes hashes; parallel might be ok. but CPU-bound hashing can blow up. if we shrink fixture, it’s fine.

Jordan Lee: also we can short-circuit hashing in tests by using smaller artifacts, as discussed.

Marcus Lin: just don’t accidentally create tests that pass even if verification skips hashing. we should keep a test that ensures hash mismatch is caught.

Ethan Park: yep.

Sahana Iyer: what about caching computed hashes? manifest already contains expected hashes. verification computes actual hashes. but for test runtime, we can keep artifact sizes small.

Devon Price: also: our “base bundle” generator currently re-hashes *every file* even when we only need a few artifacts for the integration tests.

Devon Price: could create a “test bundle builder” that emits minimal layout:
- manifest.json
- manifest.sig
- charts/ (tiny)
- binaries/ (tiny)
- sbom/ pointer file

Connor O'Brien: +1. our CI should validate semantics, not the full production payload.

Sahana Iyer: ok. action items:
- Ethan: refactor suite to build base bundle once per package; use hardlink copy per test + break link on mutated file
- Devon: swap heavy fixtures (docker save) -> deterministic tiny tar/file
- Sahana: adjust Buildkite step timeouts + add timing logs (per phase)

Ethan Park: i’ll take the refactor. estimate: today + tomorrow.

Devon Price: i’ll patch fixtures now. probably behind a flag so we don’t lose the “realistic” path names.

Marcus Lin: please add a comment in code about why fixtures are small (perf) + what security properties are still validated.

Sean Gallagher: once it’s down, can we set an SLO for this job? like <12m p95 on main.

Sahana Iyer: yep. will add to infra dashboard after we stabilize.

Devon Price: quick note: deterministic tar creation should use:
`tar --sort=name --mtime='UTC 2020-01-01' --owner=0 --group=0 --numeric-owner -cf ...`
so hashes are stable.

Jordan Lee: love it. also ensure json canonicalization for manifest is stable. our tests currently write json with stdlib which doesn’t guarantee key order if map-based.

Ethan Park: base bundle generator uses our canonical writer (sorted keys). good.

Connor O'Brien: ping me if we need to change the release gate thresholds. but ideally this doesn’t block RC builds anymore.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118301 started for redwood-private-installer / branch `ethan/shared-bundle-fixture`

Sahana Iyer: thanks all. let’s regroup after we have numbers.

Ethan Park: update: refactor done locally. base bundle built once. tamper tests run in parallel with isolated dirs. runtime on my laptop: 4m -> 55s.

Devon Price: fixture patch landed too. docker save removed. the “image tar” is now 8KB deterministic file. :thumbsup:

Marcus Lin: reviewed the changes. looks fine. keep one test that ensures changing `images/redwood-runtime.tar` triggers `HASH_MISMATCH` and we’re good.

Sahana Iyer: awesome. once CI confirms, i’ll tighten timeout back down and add an alert if it regresses.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118301 passed
ci-bot: Step timings:
- bundle-verify-integration: 6m12s (was 33m41s)

Sahana Iyer: ✅ perf is back. i’ll file a follow-up to add a nightly “full-size bundle smoke” so we still catch weird tar/unpack issues without slowing main.

Connor O'Brien: perfect. this unblocks the release train gate. thanks.

title: #architecture: 1759953233
preview:
Rafael Mendes: pulling in #architecture folks for a decision we keep circling. where does the source-of-truth for the public status page component registry live? (component ids, display names, owner, mapping rules, rollout flags). options: repo config vs Confluence page.

Christine Wong: +1. current pain is drift: confluence has “proposed” list, incident-bot has a partial copy, dashboards use slightly diff names.

Irene Choi: repo wins by default for “source of truth” if it’s consumed by tooling. confluence should be a rendered view, not authoritative.

Allison Grant: agree in principle. from observability side: we need the registry to drive dashboard generation + alert labels. we can validate in CI if it’s in git.

Miles Thornton: if it’s in Confluence, the only “validation” is social. also harder to roll back a bad change mid-incident.

Rafael Mendes: concerns I’ve heard:
- oncall needs an easy way to see/edit component list
- product/docs want a human-readable canonical description
- security/legal wants review for anything that changes customer-facing language

Ethan Park: oncall editing in Confluence sounds nice until it breaks automation. we should not be editing customer-facing components during an incident anyway (other than status updates). component taxonomy changes should be planned.

Dev Patel: from Support POV, the main thing is: if the public labels change, support macros and customer comms need to match. having it in git is fine as long as there’s an approval step / heads up.

Logan Wright: if we put it in repo, we can generate the confluence page from it (or at least link it). but the source should be machine-readable.

Christine Wong: what repo? incident-bot repo? observability-pack? main redwood mono?

Allison Grant: prefer a neutral/shared place. observability-pack already has dashboard config + the schema work in flight. also ties to the “signals driving status” standard.

Devon Price: incident-bot just consumes it. i’d rather not host the registry there.

Mina Haddad: needs to be accessible to routing/region taxonomy too. if it’s in observability-pack, is that okay for runtime/platform folks?

Irene Choi: yes if we do proper CODEOWNERS and reviews. we already have cross-team config in repos.

Rafael Mendes: proposal:
1) source-of-truth registry in git (observability-pack) as `statuspage/component-registry.json`
2) JSON schema + CI validation (ids stable, required fields present, no dup display names per region group, etc)
3) Confluence page becomes “published view” that mirrors the registry + additional narrative, but explicitly NOT canonical

Miles Thornton: +1 and add: keep a changelog section in the file header or a `CHANGELOG.md` so we can answer “when did this component split happen?”

Elliot Price: docs angle: if the definitions are in JSON, we can still have docs that explain “what does Hosted API – US/East mean” and link to the registry. but i don’t want docs to be blocked by code review every time we tweak wording.

Michael Grant: legal concern is not the location, it’s control. if a change modifies customer-facing language (display names, descriptions), we need an approval gate. otherwise we risk inconsistent promises.

Naomi Feldman: security/compliance: +1 to auditability. git gives an audit trail. Confluence edits are harder to lock down and review consistently.

Christine Wong: so maybe separate “immutable ids + owner + mapping” vs “long-form description”? the description field could be optional / lightweight.

Allison Grant: i’d still keep a short description in registry for UI/tooling, but not a marketing blurb. anything more verbose lives in docs/confluence.

Dev Patel: review requirement suggestion:
- any change that adds/removes a component OR changes public display_name requires SRE + Support signoff
- changes to ownership/contact can be lighter weight

Irene Choi: also need infra/platform signoff when mapping rules change (service->component). it can impact paging + dashboards.

Rafael Mendes: ok, let’s be concrete on review rules.
Draft:
- CODEOWNERS for `/statuspage/component-registry.json` includes `@eng-sre` + `@eng-platform` + `@observability`
- if `public_display_name` or `public_grouping` changes: require `@support` reviewer + ping `#support`
- if `mapping_rules` changes: require `@eng-platform` reviewer (and `@incident-bot` as FYI)
- if `description` changes only: regular CODEOWNERS ok, but keep it factual

Miles Thornton: add a “rollout flag” in registry (e.g., `enabled: false` until dashboards + incident-bot are ready). prevents partial launch.

Devon Price: +1. incident-bot can read only enabled components.

Logan Wright: CI checks could include “no enabled component without dashboard link” to avoid half-wired entries.

Christine Wong: how do we handle Confluence? i can update the existing taxonomy page to say “generated from registry” and link to the file + ADR.

Elliot Price: i can work with Wei to adjust wording in templates to refer to component ids consistently. but please don’t make Confluence the place people go to copy/paste names.

Naomi Feldman: request: document who can approve emergency changes and how to revert. i’d rather we never do it, but reality.

Rafael Mendes: emergency changes: treat as normal PR with expedited reviewers. no direct pushes. if Sev0 and we absolutely must correct a mislabel, IC can request fast-track review from oncall SRE + one of (Infra/Obs). but ideally we only change status updates, not taxonomy.

Michael Grant: that’s acceptable. add note: any change that alters customer-facing wording during an active incident needs comms lead awareness.

Allison Grant: we should capture all of this in an ADR so it’s not “Slack law.”

Rafael Mendes: yep, i’ll write ADR-014 today with:
- decision: source-of-truth in git (observability-pack)
- confluence is informational mirror
- review requirements + CODEOWNERS
- rollout + validation strategy

Christine Wong: can we also explicitly state: component IDs never change once enabled? only add new + deprecate old.

Irene Choi: +1. stable ids are key for metrics continuity.

Dev Patel: and deprecation should have a window (e.g., 30 days) so support can update macros.

Miles Thornton: (reactions: :thumbsup: x4)

Rafael Mendes: sounds like consensus: repo as source-of-truth, Confluence as mirror. i’ll post ADR draft in this thread for final comments.

Allison Grant: works. once ADR lands, we can unblock the schema PR + mapping work.

Devon Price: +1 please tag me on ADR. i’ll align incident-bot consumption to new path.

Christine Wong: i’ll update the taxonomy page with a banner once ADR is approved.

Rafael Mendes: closing the loop: target to finalize ADR by EOD tomorrow so ENG-4821 can proceed without ambiguity.

Christine Wong: so maybe separate “immutable ids + owner + mapping” vs “long-form description”? the description field could be optional / lightweight.

Allison Grant: i’d still keep a short description in registry for UI/tooling, but not a marketing blurb. anything more verbose lives in docs/confluence.

Dev Patel: review requirement suggestion:
- any change that adds/removes a component OR changes public display_name requires SRE + Support signoff
- changes to ownership/contact can be lighter weight

Irene Choi: also need infra/platform signoff when mapping rules change (service->component). it can impact paging + dashboards.

Rafael Mendes: ok, let’s be concrete on review rules.
Draft:
- CODEOWNERS for `/statuspage/component-registry.json` includes `@eng-sre` + `@eng-platform` + `@observability`
- if `public_display_name` or `public_grouping` changes: require `@support` reviewer + ping `#support`
- if `mapping_rules` changes: require `@eng-platform` reviewer (and `@incident-bot` as FYI)
- if `description` changes only: regular CODEOWNERS ok, but keep it factual

Miles Thornton: add a “rollout flag” in registry (e.g., `enabled: false` until dashboards + incident-bot are ready). prevents partial launch.

Devon Price: +1. incident-bot can read only enabled components.

Logan Wright: CI checks could include “no enabled component without dashboard link” to avoid half-wired entries.

Christine Wong: how do we handle Confluence? i can update the existing taxonomy page to say “generated from registry” and link to the file + ADR.

Elliot Price: i can work with Wei to adjust wording in templates to refer to component ids consistently. but please don’t make Confluence the place people go to copy/paste names.

Naomi Feldman: request: document who can approve emergency changes and how to revert. i’d rather we never do it, but reality.

Rafael Mendes: emergency changes: treat as normal PR with expedited reviewers. no direct pushes. if Sev0 and we absolutely must correct a mislabel, IC can request fast-track review from oncall SRE + one of (Infra/Obs). but ideally we only change status updates, not taxonomy.

Michael Grant: that’s acceptable. add note: any change that alters customer-facing wording during an active incident needs comms lead awareness.

Allison Grant: we should capture all of this in an ADR so it’s not “Slack law.”

Rafael Mendes: yep, i’ll write ADR-014 today with:
- decision: source-of-truth in git (observability-pack)
- confluence is informational mirror
- review requirements + CODEOWNERS
- rollout + validation strategy

Christine Wong: can we also explicitly state: component IDs never change once enabled? only add new + deprecate old.

Irene Choi: +1. stable ids are key for metrics continuity.

Dev Patel: and deprecation should have a window (e.g., 30 days) so support can update macros.

Miles Thornton: (reactions: :thumbsup: x4)

Rafael Mendes: sounds like consensus: repo as source-of-truth, Confluence as mirror. i’ll post ADR draft in this thread for final comments.

Allison Grant: works. once ADR lands, we can unblock the schema PR + mapping work.

Devon Price: +1 please tag me on ADR. i’ll align incident-bot consumption to new path.

Christine Wong: i’ll update the taxonomy page with a banner once ADR is approved.

Rafael Mendes: closing the loop: target to finalize ADR by EOD tomorrow so ENG-4821 can proceed without ambiguity.

title: #eng-ml: 1756661234
preview:
Ethan Park: spun up fennel-embed-v1.1-small in canary this morning. topline: overall recall@10 ~-1% vs prod, but recall@1 on very short contexts (1-3 tokens) down ~8%. :frown:
Maya Patel: interesting, is pooling change involved? v1.1 switched from mean-pool->attn-pool right?
Ethan Park: yes, we introduced a lightweight attention pooler to boost long-contexts. seems like short contexts lost signal.
Liam Chen: any token norm diffs or embedding table scale shifts? short queries often dominated by single token norms.
Ethan Park: ran a quick token-norm diff vs prod: mean L2 per-token up ~12% for top 20 short tokens. uploaded CSV to artifacts: https://artifacts.redwood/fennel/token_norms.csv
Rina Das: did you also test CLS token fallback? sometimes atten-pool weights CLS less for tiny inputs.
Ethan Park: good point. ran a small comparison: mean-pool recall@1=42.3%, attn-pool=36.1%, cls-token=45.0% (but cls embedding quality inconsistent across docs).
Maya Patel: cls doing better on 1-3 tokens suggests attention head biases. can you dump attention weights for short examples?
Ethan Park: sure, sample dump: ```json
{"q":"ok","attn_weights":[0.02,0.97,0.01]}
{"q":"api","attn_weights":[0.90,0.05,0.05]}
```
Liam Chen: that first example shows attention putting mass on a stop token middle token. could be positional bias. also check post-normalization step. did you L2-normalize final embeddings?
Ethan Park: yes final L2 norm applied in the pipeline. disabling it doesn't help much.
Rina Das: which index config? HNSW ef/search? ANN pressure can surface as recall@1 noise for tiny vectors.
Ethan Park: HNSW index, ef=200, ef_search=128, same as prod. tried ef_search=512 no change.
Maya Patel: did you try mean-centering token vectors before pool? fixes similar issues before.
Ethan Park: tried mean-center token-level and then attn-pool: recall@1 improved from 36.1% -> 40.8%. still shy of prod 44.5%.
(eval-bot): quick-eval run: `python3 bench.py --model fennel-embed-v1.1 --dataset canary-shorttail --k 1,5,10 --pool attn --postproc mean_center_tokens=true` 
(eval-bot): results summary: recall@1 40.8%, recall@5 68.9%, recall@10 77.5%. baseline prod recall@1 44.5%.
Liam Chen: sounds like two levers: 1) adjust attn-pool params so it doesn't underweight single-token cues, 2) small contrastive calibration to pull up short-token centroids.
Rina Das: agree. also check training token dropout/sentence length mix—maybe v1.1 had more long-examples during fine-tune.
Ethan Park: noted. I'm going to run a micro-calib: 1e-4 LR contrastive pass on 100k short examples, freeze encoder, only scale-term on token table. will report.
Maya Patel: if calibration helps, we can ship a targeted postproc flag while we re-train a balanced v1.1a. also add a canary traffic test on 2% before wider rollout.
Liam Chen: please add an experiment to test attn temperature sweep (0.1,0.5,1.0) to see sensitivity.
Ethan Park: added tasks in the issue: https://github.com/redwood/infra/issues/1421. will push eval logs to the issue.
Rina Das: one more ask—run silhouette score on short-query cluster vs prod, helps quantify fragmentation.
(eval-bot): silhouette quick: prod=0.42, fennel-v1.1 attn=0.31, attn+mean-center=0.36.
Ethan Park: thanks all. I'll post the calibration results later today and propose either small calibration + postproc or revert to mean-pool for tiny contexts.
Maya Patel: nice triage. keep notes on any quantized runs too (int8 tends to amplify token-norm issues).
Ethan Park: will do, I already tried ONNX int8 and that amplified recall@1 drop to ~-12%. including those logs in the issue. :white_check_mark:

title: #devex: 1751239876
preview:
Maya Singh: Hey folks — landing my first tiny PR to bump a handful of deps in package-builder. quick questions: branch naming, commit format, and any repo-specific checks I should run?

Ethan Park: Welcome Maya! Branches: <type>/<linear>-short-desc or <type>/<ticket>-short. We use chore for deps. e.g. chore/4287-bump-psutil.

Maya Singh: Good to know. Commit message style? Do we keep multiple commits or squash?

Helen (eng): We prefer a single clean commit for dependency bumps. Use the conventional style: chore(deps): bump psutil 5.8.0 -> 5.9.1. If there were notable behavior changes add a short body.

Maya Singh: Where's the PR template and label guidance?

Ethan Park: PR template: .github/pull_request_template.md. Add labels: area/build and label:needs-review. For dep bumps include a migration/compat note if needed.

ProtoBot: FYI: If you modify any proto files, update generated files under proto-gen and run `scripts/gen_protos.sh`.

Maya Singh: Thanks — no protos here. CI failed earlier on my branch with a license header complaint even though pre-commit passed locally. Any common root cause?

Helen (eng): License header checks are enforced by pre-commit hook but CI uses an older hook cache sometimes. Run `pre-commit run license-header --all-files` or `scripts/apply_license_headers`. Also ensure your editor didn't strip the comment start.

Maya Singh: Got it. What about mypy/untyped code? I see occasional CI mypy failures for other PRs.

Ethan Park: We run mypy in CI with strict config for certain packages. Prefer adding narrow typing or # type: ignore with a short comment if a third-party stubs are missing. Locally run `tox -e py39-mypy` to match CI.

Maya Singh: Any perf checks for build-related changes?

Luis: For packaging/deps, not usually. If you touch runtime libs (numpy, torch) ping @luis and run the quick smoke harness scripts/bench/smoke.sh.

Maya Singh: Good point — none of those here. Pre-merge checklist I should follow?

Ethan Park: Quick checklist: 1) run pre-commit and tests 2) make sure changelog (if user-facing) is updated 3) link Linear ticket in PR body 4) add reviewers (owner + one peer)

Maya Singh: Who are the owners for package-builder?

Helen (eng): Tag @ethan and @helen. Owner files are in CODEOWNERS. If unsure, request an informal review from the oncall.

CI Bot: build/1751239876 — status: running (lint, unit tests, mypy) :hourglass:

Maya Singh: One small nit — do we prefer rebasing onto main before merge?

Ethan Park: Yes, rebase to keep main linear. If there are merge conflicts, resolve on your branch and re-run CI.

Maya Singh: I bumped three transitive deps in subpackage. Anything else I should include in the PR body?

Helen (eng): Add a short testing note: which tests you ran locally and CI artifacts to watch for. Also mark the PR as non-breaking if applicable.

CI Bot: build/1751239876 — status: success (lint, unit tests) :white_check_mark:

Maya Singh: All green. Sending for review now — thanks for the quick onboarding help! :tada:

Ethan Park: Welcome aboard Maya, and congrats on the first merged PR. Small thing — after merge, run `make verify-ci` once to ensure branch protection didn't block anything.

Maya Singh: Will do — thanks everyone!

title: #eng-infra: 1748572103
preview:
Vanessa Ortiz: kicking off a quick thread to de-risk bounds implementation before we merge the autoscaler changes. Need alignment on (1) edge cases + semantics (2) defaulting strategy for existing Dedicated deployments (3) who owns migration/backfill. I’m looking at ENG-22140 + ENG-22153.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1. I’m in the autoscaler loop code now. Biggest question: do we expect control plane to always send explicit `min_replicas` / `max_replicas`, or can they be null? i can clamp either way but need deterministic behavior.

Benji Okafor: schema PR (ENG-22141) is leaning nullable fields: `bounds_enabled` + `min_replicas?` + `max_replicas?` + cooldowns. Validation: if bounds_enabled=true then require both min/max and enforce min<=max. If bounds_enabled=false, we treat min/max as ignored even if present.

Jordan Lee: from policy engine POV, we can compute an “effective policy” snapshot that the autoscaler reads. Proposal:
- if feature flag off OR bounds_enabled=false => `effective_min`/`effective_max` = empty, autoscaler uses legacy behavior
- if bounds_enabled=true => clamp desired replicas into [min,max]
We should also emit a reason string for audit/logging: `clamp:min` / `clamp:max` / `no-bounds`.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok but we still need to decide what happens to *existing* deployments when we first roll out schema. we can’t suddenly require min/max everywhere.

Michael Nguyen: on migration/backfill: I can add columns/fields with null defaults and backfill policy rows in a “legacy mode” so reads don’t break. But we need to decide what value to set for existing deployments in DB.

Ethan Park: strong preference: do NOT backfill min/max for existing customers. Too risky (we’ll guess wrong). Instead:
- add new policy object w/ bounds_enabled=false by default
- only set bounds_enabled=true when explicitly enabled via console/api + feature flag
That preserves behavior.

Keiko Yamamoto: works for me as long as autoscaler treats “no policy / legacy policy” identically. I’ll code defensively: if policy missing or bounds_enabled=false => skip clamp.

Rafael Mendes: SRE lens: please avoid any migration that changes fleet behavior without explicit enablement. If we do backfill, it should be a no-op. Also we need a clear story on “max below current replicas” if a customer tightens bounds.

Vanessa Ortiz: yeah, edge cases list i want to cover:
1) min > max (should be rejected)
2) max < current_replicas on update
3) min > current_replicas on update
4) min=0 (do we allow scale-to-zero?)
5) predictor missing/stale; predictive wants 0 or null
6) region shortage: can’t meet min

Benji Okafor: (1) will be API validation error 400 w/ field-level message.
Re (4) min=0: I’d allow only if deployment has `allow_scale_to_zero=true` (we don’t expose that broadly for Dedicated today). Otherwise enforce min>=1.

Jordan Lee: +1. For Dedicated, scale-to-zero is basically never appropriate (baseline SLO + warm cache). We can have `min_replicas >= 1` as validation for Dedicated deployments.

Keiko Yamamoto: (2) if max < current, what’s desired? immediate downscale to max? or just prevent scale-ups and let reactive scale-down eventually bring it down?

Rafael Mendes: please do not immediately drop capacity on config change unless user explicitly asked and we can do it safely. But also: the customer *is* setting a hard ceiling, so we can’t keep running above max indefinitely.

Ethan Park: semantics I’d propose:
- ceiling is enforced on scale-up decisions immediately (never increase above max)
- if currently above max, autoscaler should schedule a controlled scale-down toward max, subject to cooldown + “safe scale-down” constraints (no step-down bigger than X per interval)
We should document that it may take up to N minutes to converge.

Vanessa Ortiz: i like that. Keiko can you implement “converge down to max” with dampening? We already have cooldown plumbing in the loop.

Keiko Yamamoto: yep. I’ll add:
- if current > max: treat desired = max (then pass through existing scale-down guards + cooldown)
- step size cap on downscale (e.g. 20% or at least 1 replica)
- emit `bound_exceeded_current` metric so dashboard shows “running above max” state.

Samantha Roy: for telemetry: can we standardize metrics now? I can wire:
- `autoscaler_desired_replicas`
- `autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas`
- `autoscaler_bound_hit_total{bound=min|max}`
- `autoscaler_current_above_max` gauge
Need labels: customer_id, deployment_id, region (with cardinality guard).

Vanessa Ortiz: yes, this thread is exactly to pin that down. Also (6) region shortage: if min cannot be met, autoscaler will keep requesting min, but scheduler may not place. We need degraded surfacing.

Rafael Mendes: + alert. “min unmet for >10m” is customer-impacting. But make sure we don’t page on transient placement delays.

Jordan Lee: policy engine can include a `min_unmet_behavior` later, but for v1 simplest:
- autoscaler continues to request min
- control plane marks deployment status `DEGRADED_MIN_UNMET` when provisioning fails with capacity shortage signal
- emit event + metric

Michael Nguyen: ownership question: who’s writing the backfill job? I can handle DB migration + backfill to set `bounds_enabled=false` for all existing Dedicated deployments. But I need the canonical definition of “existing deployment has no record” vs “record w/ bounds_enabled=false” for the autoscaler.

Keiko Yamamoto: from my side either works. If no record: treat as legacy. If record exists but bounds_enabled=false: treat as legacy. Just don’t 500.

Ethan Park: let’s do explicit record on backfill so console reads something consistent. But content should be equivalent to legacy behavior. i.e. bounds_enabled=false, predictive_enabled=false, cooldown fields default to current platform defaults.

Benji Okafor: careful: if we backfill cooldowns, we might accidentally change tuning. Suggest leaving cooldown fields null too and have the autoscaler apply its legacy defaults unless bounds_enabled/predictive_enabled flips on.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. legacy defaults should stay in autoscaler config for now. Only apply policy cooldowns when enabled.

Michael Nguyen: ok, plan:
- migration adds new policy table/fields
- backfill creates policy rows for existing Dedicated deployments with `bounds_enabled=false`, `predictive_enabled=false`, other fields null
- also write an audit log event `scaling_policy_initialized`? or skip to avoid noise?

Rafael Mendes: skip audit events for backfill IMO (or mark actor as `system` and suppress customer-visible export). Compliance folks might want it recorded but not spammy.

Jordan Lee: we can do: actor=`system/migration`, reason=`schema_backfill`, and in export pipeline we can filter those from default customer views. (Kevin Osei asked for that pattern in another thread.)

Vanessa Ortiz: good. last big one: predictor returning null/stale. We’ve seen “predictor says 0” in earlier prototypes. We need safe behavior.

Keiko Yamamoto: I’ll implement: if predictive_enabled=true and predictor is missing/stale/null => fall back to reactive desired (no predictive bias). Also NEVER treat null as 0. (I’ll add unit test; this bit me before :/)

Ethan Park: can we make that deterministic and logged? i.e. reason `prediction_unavailable_fallback=reactive`.

Samantha Roy: yep, I can add structured log field `prediction_state={ok|stale|null|error}`.

Jamal Pierce: on defaulting: do we want a “recommended range” shown in Console even if bounds disabled? Product is asking. That’s separate from enforcement, but we should not conflate recommended with hard bounds.

Vanessa Ortiz: agreed: recommended range is UI only. hard bounds are enforcement. We’ll keep them separate.

Rafael Mendes: action items:
- Vanessa/Keiko: confirm semantics doc for edge cases (max < current, min unmet)
- Michael: own migration/backfill implementation (ENG-22153)
- Benji/Jordan: lock validation rules (min>=1 for Dedicated, min<=max, bounds_enabled gating)
- Samantha: metric names + labels in line w/ observability standards

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: i’ll summarize in ENG-22140 and link this thread. Keiko, once you have a PR up, tag me + Rafael for review (esp. the “currently above max converge down” behavior).

Keiko Yamamoto: will do. aiming EOD tomorrow for the clamp + metrics + edge-case tests.

Michael Nguyen: starting migration branch now. I’ll coordinate with Benji on schema shape so I don’t drift.

Benji Okafor: i’ll drop the latest protobuf/json schema snippet here once it’s in review.

Vanessa Ortiz: thanks all.
