Re (4) min=0: I’d allow only if deployment has `allow_scale_to_zero=true` (we don’t expose that broadly for Dedicated today). Otherwise enforce min>=1.

Jordan Lee: +1. For Dedicated, scale-to-zero is basically never appropriate (baseline SLO + warm cache). We can have `min_replicas >= 1` as validation for Dedicated deployments.

Keiko Yamamoto: (2) if max < current, what’s desired? immediate downscale to max? or just prevent scale-ups and let reactive scale-down eventually bring it down?

Rafael Mendes: please do not immediately drop capacity on config change unless user explicitly asked and we can do it safely. But also: the customer *is* setting a hard ceiling, so we can’t keep running above max indefinitely.

Ethan Park: semantics I’d propose:
- ceiling is enforced on scale-up decisions immediately (never increase above max)
- if currently above max, autoscaler should schedule a controlled scale-down toward max, subject to cooldown + “safe scale-down” constraints (no step-down bigger than X per interval)
We should document that it may take up to N minutes to converge.

Vanessa Ortiz: i like that. Keiko can you implement “converge down to max” with dampening? We already have cooldown plumbing in the loop.

Keiko Yamamoto: yep. I’ll add:
- if current > max: treat desired = max (then pass through existing scale-down guards + cooldown)
- step size cap on downscale (e.g. 20% or at least 1 replica)
- emit `bound_exceeded_current` metric so dashboard shows “running above max” state.

Samantha Roy: for telemetry: can we standardize metrics now? I can wire:
- `autoscaler_desired_replicas`
- `autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas`
- `autoscaler_bound_hit_total{bound=min|max}`
- `autoscaler_current_above_max` gauge
Need labels: customer_id, deployment_id, region (with cardinality guard).

Vanessa Ortiz: yes, this thread is exactly to pin that down. Also (6) region shortage: if min cannot be met, autoscaler will keep requesting min, but scheduler may not place. We need degraded surfacing.

Rafael Mendes: + alert. “min unmet for >10m” is customer-impacting. But make sure we don’t page on transient placement delays.

Jordan Lee: policy engine can include a `min_unmet_behavior` later, but for v1 simplest:
- autoscaler continues to request min
- control plane marks deployment status `DEGRADED_MIN_UNMET` when provisioning fails with capacity shortage signal
- emit event + metric

Michael Nguyen: ownership question: who’s writing the backfill job? I can handle DB migration + backfill to set `bounds_enabled=false` for all existing Dedicated deployments. But I need the canonical definition of “existing deployment has no record” vs “record w/ bounds_enabled=false” for the autoscaler.

Keiko Yamamoto: from my side either works. If no record: treat as legacy. If record exists but bounds_enabled=false: treat as legacy. Just don’t 500.

Ethan Park: let’s do explicit record on backfill so console reads something consistent. But content should be equivalent to legacy behavior. i.e. bounds_enabled=false, predictive_enabled=false, cooldown fields default to current platform defaults.

Benji Okafor: careful: if we backfill cooldowns, we might accidentally change tuning. Suggest leaving cooldown fields null too and have the autoscaler apply its legacy defaults unless bounds_enabled/predictive_enabled flips on.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. legacy defaults should stay in autoscaler config for now. Only apply policy cooldowns when enabled.

Michael Nguyen: ok, plan:
- migration adds new policy table/fields
- backfill creates policy rows for existing Dedicated deployments with `bounds_enabled=false`, `predictive_enabled=false`, other fields null
- also write an audit log event `scaling_policy_initialized`? or skip to avoid noise?

Rafael Mendes: skip audit events for backfill IMO (or mark actor as `system` and suppress customer-visible export). Compliance folks might want it recorded but not spammy.

Jordan Lee: we can do: actor=`system/migration`, reason=`schema_backfill`, and in export pipeline we can filter those from default customer views. (Kevin Osei asked for that pattern in another thread.)

Vanessa Ortiz: good. last big one: predictor returning null/stale. We’ve seen “predictor says 0” in earlier prototypes. We need safe behavior.

Keiko Yamamoto: I’ll implement: if predictive_enabled=true and predictor is missing/stale/null => fall back to reactive desired (no predictive bias). Also NEVER treat null as 0. (I’ll add unit test; this bit me before :/)

Ethan Park: can we make that deterministic and logged? i.e. reason `prediction_unavailable_fallback=reactive`.

Samantha Roy: yep, I can add structured log field `prediction_state={ok|stale|null|error}`.

Jamal Pierce: on defaulting: do we want a “recommended range” shown in Console even if bounds disabled? Product is asking. That’s separate from enforcement, but we should not conflate recommended with hard bounds.

Vanessa Ortiz: agreed: recommended range is UI only. hard bounds are enforcement. We’ll keep them separate.

Rafael Mendes: action items:
- Vanessa/Keiko: confirm semantics doc for edge cases (max < current, min unmet)
- Michael: own migration/backfill implementation (ENG-22153)
- Benji/Jordan: lock validation rules (min>=1 for Dedicated, min<=max, bounds_enabled gating)
- Samantha: metric names + labels in line w/ observability standards

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: i’ll summarize in ENG-22140 and link this thread. Keiko, once you have a PR up, tag me + Rafael for review (esp. the “currently above max converge down” behavior).

Keiko Yamamoto: will do. aiming EOD tomorrow for the clamp + metrics + edge-case tests.

Michael Nguyen: starting migration branch now. I’ll coordinate with Benji on schema shape so I don’t drift.

Benji Okafor: i’ll drop the latest protobuf/json schema snippet here once it’s in review.

Vanessa Ortiz: thanks all.

title: #eng: 1747333303
preview:
Priya Natarajan: We’re close to flipping the oncall handoff for the PLG signal router. Can folks drop the *must-have* metrics + alert conditions? Thinking HubSpot 429s, retries, dropped events, lag, etc. Also owners + dashboard link placeholders so we can wire this into PagerDuty.

Logan Wright: +1. From telemetry/analytics side, the top things that will bite us:
- event lag (telemetry -> analytics table -> scoring output)
- aggregator job failures / partial writes
- dropped events / DLQ growth
If we don’t alert on lag, sales will just see “nothing happening” and assume routing is broken.

Mei Lin: For the daily aggregation job (ENG-7732 / PR-28458), we should alert on:
- `aggregation_job_success` (1/0) per run
- `aggregation_rows_written` (unexpectedly low vs trailing 7d baseline)
- `aggregation_backfill_runs` (if backfill is running, suppress some lag alerts)
Also a gauge for `analytics_table_partition_freshness_hours`.

Caleb Johnson: Ingestion/data quality:
- `telemetry_event_ingest_lag_seconds` p95 and max
- `telemetry_events_dropped_total` (with reason label: schema_invalid, auth, throttled)
- `telemetry_events_dlq_depth`
We should page only on sustained DLQ growth + lag, not on occasional schema_invalid spikes (docs instrumentation can be noisy).

Ethan Park: HubSpot integration metrics (PR-28503 + PR-28544):
- `hubspot_requests_total` by endpoint
- `hubspot_429s_total` + `hubspot_rate_limited_seconds_total`
- `hubspot_5xx_total`
- `hubspot_retries_total` + `hubspot_retry_queue_depth`
- `hubspot_task_create_failures_total`
- `hubspot_company_upsert_failures_total`
Also *idempotency* issues are subtle — a counter for `hubspot_deduped_updates_total` would help validate we’re not spamming timeline events.

Nadia Rahman: Scoring service should expose:
- `signals_created_total` (by signal_type)
- `signals_suppressed_total` (by suppression_reason)
- `signals_routed_total` (by destination=hubspot)
- `signals_failed_total` (validation_failed, missing_company_mapping, hubspot_error)
And a histogram: `signal_end_to_end_latency_seconds` (telemetry timestamp -> task created)

Marcus Lin: From security side: please add a metric + alert if we ever attempt to send disallowed fields to HubSpot.
Like `hubspot_payload_policy_violations_total` (should be 0 always). If >0, page the oncall + auto-disable routing.

Talia Benson: Capturing this for the runbook. Can we agree on *alert thresholds* (page vs ticket)? Proposed:
P0 page:
- HubSpot 429s sustained (>= 10% of requests for 10m) OR retry queue depth continuously increasing for 15m
- End-to-end lag p95 > 6h (excluding backfills)
- Task create failures > 2% for 10m
P1 ticket:
- 5xx spikes but recovering
- row writes drop >50% vs 7d median
Thoughts?

Logan Wright: 6h lag feels high given we’re doing daily windows, but the expectation from sales is “I should see tasks same day.” I’d set:
- ticket at p95 > 2h for 2 consecutive hours
- page at p95 > 6h for 2 consecutive hours
and explicitly suppress during backfill.

Mei Lin: +1 to suppression during backfills. We can tag runs with `window_type=daily|backfill` and key alerts off daily only.
Also add:
- `aggregation_window_complete_timestamp` gauge (epoch) so we can compute freshness in Grafana easily.

Ethan Park: On 429s: HubSpot rate limits are bursty. I’d rather page on “routing stopped” symptoms:
- `hubspot_task_create_success_rate` < 95% for 15m (page)
- `hubspot_retry_queue_depth` > 5k and increasing (page)
And a warning when 429 ratio > 5% for 10m (ticket) so we tune batching.

Caleb Johnson: For dropped events: page if `telemetry_events_dropped_total{reason!=schema_invalid}` increases by > 1k in 10m AND lag p95 > 1h. Otherwise we’ll page every time a client SDK rolls.

Priya Natarajan: This is great. Let’s lock a minimal set for day-1 oncall:
1) HubSpot delivery health (success rate, retries, 429/5xx)
2) E2E lag
3) DLQ depth / dropped events
4) Job/run success
Anything beyond that can be phase 2.

Nadia Rahman: Please also include a “volume sanity” panel:
- `signals_created_total` by type
- `signals_routed_total` by region
If we suddenly double our routing volume, that’s usually a threshold regression.

Talia Benson: Owners?
- HubSpot metrics/alerts: @Ethan Park
- Ingestion lag/DLQ/drops: @Caleb Johnson
- Aggregation freshness + writes: @Mei Lin
- Overall dashboards + PD integration: I can coordinate but need a DRI for the service

Priya Natarajan: DRI for service ops is Logan (Telemetry & Analytics) for this pilot. Ethan owns HubSpot path. I’ll be escalation for platform.

DeployBot: reminder: PR-28630 (metrics hooks) merged to main. Staging deploy scheduled 18:00 PT.

Logan Wright: Dashboard placeholders (will fill once Grafana panels are live):
- Signal Router Overview: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/overview (TBD)
- HubSpot Delivery: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/hubspot (TBD)
- Ingestion + DLQ: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/ingestion (TBD)
Runbook placeholder: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENG/PLG+Signal+Router+Oncall+Runbook (TBD)

Marcus Lin: +1 on runbook including “kill switch” steps if payload policy violations fire.

Priya Natarajan: Action items:
- Ethan: define alert rules for HubSpot success rate + retry queue depth (and add to PD service)
- Mei/Caleb: freshness + DLQ alerts with backfill suppression
- Talia: create runbook skeleton + link to INT-1843 + suppression guidance
Let’s aim to have alerts in place before we expand pilot cohort next week.

title: #customer-success: 1742100000
preview:
Priya Rao: @here onboarding customer Luca ran into 429s after switching from single-threaded calls to our Python SDK batch helper. Customer is blocked on finish of integration. Paste of error:

Luca Marino: "got HTTP 429 when posting to /v1/generate, no retry header"

Priya Rao: Logs show repeated 429s immediately after they turned on parallel workers (8 -> 40). stack trace snippet:
```
requests.exceptions.HTTPError: 429 Client Error: Too Many Requests for url: https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate
  File "app.py", line 112, in call_model
    resp = client.generate(...)
```

Janelle Kim: Which SDK version and client config? Are they using the global token or per-process token? Also any proxy / NAT that could collapse connections?

Ethan Park: common causes: (1) concurrent workers exhausting per-key rate limit, (2) missing exponential backoff, (3) client re-creating new connections for each request. Ask for their sdk config and threading model.

Priya Rao: @Luca can you paste the code snippet showing how you instantiate the client? also run `pip show redwood-sdk` and paste the version

Luca Marino: using redwood-sdk 0.6.2, code:
```
client = Redwood(api_key=os.environ['RW_API_KEY'])
for _ in range(40):
  Thread(target=lambda: client.generate(prompt)).start()
```

Marta Alvarez: aha — the SDK maintains a small connection pool; starting 40 threads each making new requests will spike concurrent inflight and hit per-second token limits. Recommend using the SDK's async batcher or reduce parallelism to ~8 and enable retries/backoff. Also we can temporarily raise their burst quota while onboarding.

Janelle Kim: also check they're not inadvertently rotating the key or re-setting env in worker threads. If they're in a container per worker, each container might be treated as a separate client and counted against short-window limits.

Priya Rao: Can we proactively offer a config snippet? Suggest:
```
client = Redwood(api_key=os.environ.get('RW_API_KEY'), max_connections=12, enable_auto_backoff=True)
# OR use async batcher:
await client.batch_generate(prompts, batch_size=16)
```

OpsBot: [automated] 1742100060 user.luca@example.com project:acme-onboard - 129 429 responses in last 5m :warning:

Luca Marino: trying reduced concurrency to 10 now...

Luca Marino: ok switching to 10 workers + backoff — errors stopped. thanks!

Priya Rao: great. Marta / Janelle — can we follow up to (1) offer temporary quota bump, (2) schedule a 30m enablement session to show best practices for batching + connection reuse, (3) capture this as onboarding notes under account health? This is a renewal risk if they hit more blockers.

Marta Alvarez: yes I can do quota bump and schedule a session tomorrow 10am PT. I'll add an onboarding note in the account docket.

Ethan Park: I'll open a short KB entry pointing to 'SDK concurrency & backoff' examples. Link me to the snippet and I'll publish.

Priya Rao: thanks team. Luca, marking as resolved for now — re-open if you see further 429s after the session. :thumbsup:

title: #customer-success: 1741513319
preview:
Jamie Chen: quick q for the demo env folks — can we reuse the sales demo tenant to onboard a customer POC? (CS team has a prospect that wants to start testing this week and asked for “the environment you showed in the demo”).

Jamie Chen: specifically: can we just hand them a key + a project in the demo org, then swap out later if they buy?

Markus Klein: pls don’t 🙏 demo tenant is “known-good / synthetic only” and we try to keep it isolated + stable for live demos.

Markus Klein: a few reasons:
- demo has seeded fake usage + synthetic traffic generator running (dashboards look realistic) — will pollute/complicate their POC measurement
- model pins + fallback policies are tuned for the demo story; we don’t want customer workflows coupled to that
- we rotate demo keys / reset state often. customers would get broken unexpectedly
- demo quotas/capacity are sized for parallel demos; a POC can accidentally starve a live demo

Jamie Chen: got it. was hoping for speed but makes sense. what’s the fastest “right way” for a POC then?

Simran Kaur: POC should be its own org/project (POC tenant) so they can bring their own prompts/data, and we can set up the right guardrails (SSO later, audit log access, etc).

Simran Kaur: if they need something today/tomorrow: we can provision a “POC sandbox org” in < 1 hr if we have:
- company name + region preference
- who needs access (emails)
- whether they need VPC/private narrative vs hosted API only

Talia Benson: +1. process-wise: drop the request in #sales-demo-oncall with “POC provisioning” in the first line, and include the above. we’ll track it (and we can help with credits / rate limits).

Ethan Park: also from security/auth side: demo tenant uses least-privilege keys meant for screen share + internal users only. we don’t want external user invites/RBAC living there. keeping demo and POC separate keeps audit story clean.

Jamie Chen: perfect. I’ll file the request in #sales-demo-oncall with details. 

Markus Klein: thanks. and if the prospect asks: the message is “demo environment is a curated sandbox for consistent demos; POC is a dedicated environment so results reflect your workload + we can align on success metrics.” :thumbsup:

Jamie Chen: love it. will use that verbatim.

title: #customer-success: 1741220440
preview:
Kimberly Park: seeing a second account complain about tool calling JSON parse errors. Do we have an official workaround we can share?

Chloe Martin: Northwind CX has examples where args come back as plain text (not JSON). Ticket is SUP-1913.

Aisha Rahman: HelioPay had trailing comma invalid JSON when streaming (SUP-1842). We rolled back a flag in us-east but not sure if it covers all cases.

Ethan Park: Workaround: 1) move tool schema/instructions earlier in prompt 2) add explicit STRICT JSON instruction 3) if possible, allow one retry when validation fails. Also we can enable `tool_args_json_mode=strict` on a per-route basis.

Kimberly Park: can someone draft a one-pager for CS? want to avoid each CSM inventing guidance.

Aisha Rahman: Ill add to known-issues page and link it here.

title: #product: 1740201122
preview:
Marissa Cole: We need to lock GA scope for Hosted canary rollout workflow this week. Beta feedback is good but we’re creeping. Two big asks surfacing: (1) automated quality gates as hard stop, (2) Dedicated/Private parity at GA. We can’t do everything. Let’s decide.

Paula Mendes: +1. For GA I’m pushing for policy guardrails + telemetry auto-stop/rollback to be non-negotiable. Quality gates as hard gate feels risky/immature unless we standardize signals + avoid false positives.

Dylan Brooks: Eng POV: Console wizard + timeline + activity log are on track if we don’t add a bunch of new gating UI. If quality gates become “required step” we have to ship: config UX, storage schema, eval signal ingestion, and a bunch of error states. That’s easily another 3–5 weeks and introduces new failure modes.

Mina Haddad: Data plane side: Hosted split enforcement is basically ready (region-aware deterministic cohorting), but adding “quality gate” that automatically rolls back based on some external score is complicated unless it’s just another metric stream. If it’s “upload eval results” manually, that’s fine; if it’s “run evals in-product” that’s a new system.

Ethan Park: Dedicated parity: the control plane schema can be compatible, but operationally Dedicated has different routing + capacity semantics. If we say “GA parity” we’ll get held to it by enterprise buyers. I’d rather frame Dedicated/Private as “schema-compatible, roadmap” for now.

Sean Gallagher: SRE stance: auto-stop/rollback on SLO breach is the safety net we can commit to for GA. Quality gates as auto-stop is scary unless we can prove low noise and have a clear playbook. We already have enough to tune for latency/5xx/timeouts per region.

Marissa Cole: Ok, but GTM is getting “safe rollouts” questions that include “quality regression.” I don’t want us to look like we’re ignoring it.

Paula Mendes: We can include it as optional/manual signal in GA: Console shows a “Quality checks” section where customer can link out / paste metric URL / attach a note, but it doesn’t block progression by default. Then we follow up with automated quality gates post-GA.

Dylan Brooks: That works. UI can support: (a) optional checklist items per step (“Run eval suite X”, “Verify CSAT”), (b) required approvals as the real gate. The policy engine supports approvals now; quality checklist is just metadata.

Elliot Price: Docs can frame it as: “GA supports SLO-based auto-stop + optional quality monitoring.” We can explicitly call out: “Automated quality gating is planned; today you can integrate via your own eval pipeline and monitor metrics alongside cohort comparison.”

Wei Chen: +1. We can add a snippet: recommended practice is to run prompt set / golden tests during soak windows and annotate rollout steps. Also: link to metrics comparison view for baseline vs canary.

Connor O'Brien: Release mgmt: if we expand scope to quality hard gate or Dedicated parity, our GA checklist will slip. Feature flag enablement plan assumes Hosted-only. Please keep scope tight.

Soojin Lee: From comms: we can position “Hosted canary rollouts GA” with a clear “What’s included / What’s next.” As long as we explicitly mention manual quality checks and upcoming automation, it won’t read as a gap.

Marissa Cole: Let’s get specific. Proposed GA scope:
- Hosted routes only (Console + API)
- Percentage-based traffic splitting with deterministic cohorting
- Guardrails: max step size, min soak time, approval gates
- Auto-stop/rollback based on SLO metrics (5xx/timeouts/latency regression)
- Audit log events + customer-visible activity timeline
- Baseline vs canary metrics comparison
Not in GA:
- Automated quality gates as a required stop condition (follow-up)
- Dedicated/Private GA parity (follow-up)
But: keep schema compatibility for future Dedicated/Private.

Paula Mendes: That matches what I think we can defend for GA. One nuance: for stop conditions, we should lock “auto-pause vs auto-rollback” defaults. SRE wanted pause by default with option to rollback, right?

Sean Gallagher: Yes. Default behavior should be auto-pause on threshold breach + require explicit action to rollback, unless customer opts into auto-rollback. We need to avoid surprise rollbacks. Also: avoid alert storms—cohort alerts should page only on sustained breach (e.g., 5–10 min) not a single blip.

Rafael Mendes: +1. And we need a clear runbook path: “rollout paused due to p95 latency regression” -> check cohort dashboards, check recent deploys, check region skew, then decide rollback/resume. Auto-rollback optional is fine.

Dylan Brooks: Console UX wise: we can add copy in the wizard: “Recommended: start with 1% and soak at least 15 min.” Also show “Auto-stop is enabled” with the specific thresholds. If quality gates are “optional checklist,” we can add a section called “Quality monitoring (optional)” with guidance.

Mina Haddad: For Dedicated parity: we should make sure config schema doesn’t hardcode “Hosted” in a way that blocks Dedicated later. But we should avoid exposing Dedicated/Private controls in UI at GA.

Ethan Park: Agree. We can implement rollout object at control plane as generic “route type” with Hosted as the first allowed type. Then later enable other types. But GA messaging must say Hosted.

Marissa Cole: Any objections to cutting “Dedicated parity at GA” explicitly? I want to ensure Sales isn’t promising it implicitly.

Soojin Lee: We’ll add a line in internal launch brief + release notes: “Dedicated/Private support is not available at GA.” Also update FAQ doc for Sales/SE.

Elliot Price: I’ll make sure docs have a “Limitations” section that states Dedicated/Private not supported yet.

Paula Mendes: For quality gates follow-up, I’m going to write a ticket that defines: what counts as a “quality metric” signal, how we’d ingest it, and how to avoid misuse/noise. For GA we’ll only support manual annotations + external dashboards.

Dylan Brooks: Works. Console team will keep the wizard flexible enough to add a real “Quality gate” step later without redesign.

Connor O'Brien: Decision needed today so I can update GA change management checklist and approvers. Are we aligned on the scope call as Marissa listed?

Marissa Cole: Yes—locking it. GA = Hosted-only + SLO guardrails + auditability/metrics. Quality gates automated + Dedicated parity are post-GA follow-ups.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: As long as SLO stop conditions are in GA and default is auto-pause.

Mina Haddad: :thumbsup: Hosted split enforcement stays the focus.

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: I’ll ensure schema wording supports future expansion but doesn’t imply parity.

Dylan Brooks: :thumbsup: Console will reflect this in the workflow copy + limitation text.

Marissa Cole: Action items:
- Paula: update PRD “Non-goals for GA” + add follow-up tickets for automated quality gates + Dedicated/Private rollout support.
- Dylan: align wizard UX text (“Quality monitoring optional”) + remove any “quality gate required” language.
- Elliot/Wei: docs limitations + recommended manual quality monitoring during soak.
- Connor/Soojin: launch brief + release notes to explicitly say Hosted-only.

Paula Mendes: Will do. Also adding note: approvals are the governance mechanism for regulated customers (Emberfin etc). Quality can be part of the approval checklist for now.

Rafael Mendes: Please loop SRE on the final threshold defaults so we can prebuild alert templates + avoid tuning chaos during GA week.

Marissa Cole: Yep. I’ll set a 30 min sync tomorrow with Paula/Sean/Rafael on defaults and tiering.

Connor O'Brien: Great. I’m updating the GA readiness checklist to match this scope and marking Dedicated parity as “post-GA.”

Soojin Lee: I’ll draft the “What’s next” section: automated quality gates + Dedicated/Private rollouts + richer automation. Sharing for review.

Elliot Price: On it. I’ll add an FAQ entry: “How do I prevent quality regressions?” -> run evals during soak, compare metrics dashboards, use approvals, rollback if needed.

Marissa Cole: Cool. Locking this thread as the scope decision record.

title: #eng-platform: 1740082033
preview:
Cole Summers: Heads up: while wiring burst metering -> billing pipeline, I’m seeing a few edge cases around retries/idempotency that we should nail down before we tell Finance “numbers are clean”.

Cole Summers: Current plan (per PRD) is we emit `BurstUsageEvent` per request w/ fields: tenant_id, region, hw_class, model, burst_gpu_ms, tokens_out, tokens_in, admitted=true/false, denial_reason. The retry problem: gateway retries on 5xx/timeout and we can double-count if we emit on both attempts.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Billing pipeline today assumes events are append-only and unique by event_id. If we can’t guarantee uniqueness, reconciliation gets ugly fast.

Ethan Park: Can we rely on existing request ids from gateway? We have `x-redwood-request-id` (uuid) + optional `x-idempotency-key` (customer supplied) in Hosted. Dedicated gateway also sets a stable request id per attempt but I don’t think it’s stable across retries yet.

Benji Okafor: For Dedicated, we have envoy generating `x-request-id` per hop; it changes on retry unless we pin it. We can add a separate header that survives retry (generated once at edge).

Jordan Lee: Also need to be clear what “retry” means for burst. Is the unit we bill for “admitted capacity” or “actually consumed GPU time”? If we bill on admit but the request never executes (timeout before dispatch), that’s a mismatch.

Cole Summers: Exactly. I think we should only meter when the request actually starts execution on a burst GPU (or when the scheduler assigns burst slot). Not on admit.

Logan Wright: From telemetry side: we can emit a “start” and “end” event (or a single end event w/ duration). But we have to keep label cardinality under control. Event id should be a field in payload, not a metric label.

Allison Grant: Please don’t shove request_id into Prometheus labels :slightly_smiling_face: We can do logs/OTel events for per-request audit and keep Prom metrics aggregate.

Cole Summers: Proposal:
1) Introduce `burst_metering_key` stable across gateway retries.
2) Billing event_id = hash(tenant_id + burst_metering_key + burst_phase)
3) Emit usage only from data plane (scheduler/runtime) once we have actual GPU-ms.

Cole Summers: For #2, “burst_phase” might just be “usage_v1” so we don’t accidentally treat later schema as dup.

Nadia Rahman: If we can get a stable key, billing pipeline can do idempotent upsert / dedupe (we currently do exactly-once-ish w/ Kafka + consumer offsets, but retries upstream break assumptions). If you give me `event_id`, we can enforce uniqueness at sink.

Rishi Malhotra: Jumping in — from Finance side we care about two things:
- Customer invoice line items match the contract definition (e.g., burst GPU-seconds or burst token surcharge).
- Daily revenue reporting matches invoiced totals (no phantom usage).
If we tell customers “burst usage is measured by GPU-seconds actually consumed” then you must not charge on admit.

Laura Bennett: +1. Also we need explainability when a customer disputes: “here are the requests/intervals that contributed.” So having per-request audit trail is good, but invoicing is aggregate.

Ethan Park: On stable key: we can add `x-redwood-billing-id` generated at edge (once) and propagated through retries. Similar to idempotency, but platform-owned.

Benji Okafor: Implementation detail: envoy retry filter doesn’t preserve generated headers by default if upstream resets? We should set it explicitly in gateway code before it hits envoy retries.

Jordan Lee: Another edge case: if admission controller returns “queued” vs “denied”, request might sit, then client times out and retries. We might end up executing both unless we cancel old one. That’s not just billing; that’s correctness.

Cole Summers: For v1 we’re doing “immediate admit/deny” (no queue) per ADR draft, right? So less risk. But we still have internal retries (data plane transient errors) that can re-run dispatch.

Ethan Park: Correct: immediate admit/deny for GA, no customer-visible queue semantics. Internal backpressure still exists but request either goes through baseline or burst or gets 429.

Nadia Rahman: Then we can scope idempotency to: same customer request retried at gateway layer, or data plane “attempts” for the same request.

Logan Wright: Data plane can generate an “execution_id” per attempt. If we have `billing_id` stable, we can guarantee only one execution contributes to billing by taking the first successful execution.

Cole Summers: Yup. Something like:
- stable `billing_id` at edge
- `attempt` increments per internal retry
- emit usage event only for `terminal_state=success|partial_success` with GPU-ms measured
- if multiple success events show up (bug), billing sink dedupes by (tenant_id, billing_id)

Allison Grant: Define what “partial_success” means for streaming. If we stream tokens and then disconnect, GPU time was still consumed. Do we bill that?

Rishi Malhotra: We generally bill for work performed. If we generated 2k tokens and customer disconnected, that’s still cost. So yes, bill what’s actually generated.

Cole Summers: That means metering should be based on server-side token counts + GPU-ms, not client receipt.

Laura Bennett: Contract language needs to be precise: “usage based on Redwood-measured compute/time and tokens generated” etc. Michael/Sofia are drafting but we should sync them on these semantics.

Jordan Lee: Another edge: denied burst but served on baseline. That should not create burst usage. But we might still emit a “denial” event for observability.

Nadia Rahman: Denial events should NOT go into billing sink unless we explicitly separate streams. Otherwise someone will accidentally sum admitted=false events.

Cole Summers: Good call. We can split:
- `BurstUsageEvent` (billable) emitted only when burst pool resources consumed
- `BurstDecisionEvent` (non-billable) for analytics/ops

Logan Wright: Please also keep schema versioned. If you change field names, downstream breaks. Add `schema_version` string.

Ethan Park: What about reconciliation with Finance reporting? Rishi asked daily revenue totals.

Nadia Rahman: We can provide a daily rollup job per tenant:
- sum(burst_gpu_ms) by tenant/day/region/hw_class
- compare to invoice line items produced by billing service
- alert if delta > threshold (e.g. 0.5% or $50)
But again, this only works if upstream events are stable/idempotent.

Cole Summers: Can we get a “replay window” expectation? If we detect a bug, can we re-export from raw logs?

Nadia Rahman: Yes, but it’s painful. If we store raw usage events in the data lake (S3) with partition by day, we can reprocess. I’d rather not rely on that for GA correctness.

Benji Okafor: Action items I see:
1) Add `x-redwood-billing-id` generation at edge + propagate through routing/data plane.
2) Update metering emitter to use billing-id for event_id.
3) Split decision vs usage events.

Allison Grant: + add a short doc in the runbook: “customer disputes burst charges” -> where to pull per-request breakdown (console export or internal debug endpoint).

Cole Summers: I’ll write up a quick design note + update ENG-4827 w/ these semantics. Also going to add a unit test: simulate retry path (same billing-id, two attempts) => exactly 1 billing record.

Rishi Malhotra: Please tag me once you have the definition in writing. Finance sign-off depends on “metering unit” + reconciliation story.

BillingPipelineBot: Reminder: billing-data-prod nightly validation runs at 02:00 UTC. Current dedupe key: `event_id`. If `event_id` is empty or not unique, duplicates will be counted.

Laura Bennett: Thanks all. Once we have the stable billing-id approach, I’m comfortable telling Legal “we can defend the measurement.”

Cole Summers: Cool. I’ll loop in Michael/Sofia separately. Closing loop here after I push the header + dedupe changes.

Jordan Lee: Also need to be clear what “retry” means for burst. Is the unit we bill for “admitted capacity” or “actually consumed GPU time”? If we bill on admit but the request never executes (timeout before dispatch), that’s a mismatch.

Cole Summers: Exactly. I think we should only meter when the request actually starts execution on a burst GPU (or when the scheduler assigns burst slot). Not on admit.

Logan Wright: From telemetry side: we can emit a “start” and “end” event (or a single end event w/ duration). But we have to keep label cardinality under control. Event id should be a field in payload, not a metric label.

Allison Grant: Please don’t shove request_id into Prometheus labels :slightly_smiling_face: We can do logs/OTel events for per-request audit and keep Prom metrics aggregate.

Cole Summers: Proposal:
1) Introduce `burst_metering_key` stable across gateway retries.
2) Billing event_id = hash(tenant_id + burst_metering_key + burst_phase)
3) Emit usage only from data plane (scheduler/runtime) once we have actual GPU-ms.

Cole Summers: For #2, “burst_phase” might just be “usage_v1” so we don’t accidentally treat later schema as dup.

Nadia Rahman: If we can get a stable key, billing pipeline can do idempotent upsert / dedupe (we currently do exactly-once-ish w/ Kafka + consumer offsets, but retries upstream break assumptions). If you give me `event_id`, we can enforce uniqueness at sink.

Rishi Malhotra: Jumping in — from Finance side we care about two things:
- Customer invoice line items match the contract definition (e.g., burst GPU-seconds or burst token surcharge).
- Daily revenue reporting matches invoiced totals (no phantom usage).
If we tell customers “burst usage is measured by GPU-seconds actually consumed” then you must not charge on admit.

Laura Bennett: +1. Also we need explainability when a customer disputes: “here are the requests/intervals that contributed.” So having per-request audit trail is good, but invoicing is aggregate.

Ethan Park: On stable key: we can add `x-redwood-billing-id` generated at edge (once) and propagated through retries. Similar to idempotency, but platform-owned.

Benji Okafor: Implementation detail: envoy retry filter doesn’t preserve generated headers by default if upstream resets? We should set it explicitly in gateway code before it hits envoy retries.

Jordan Lee: Another edge case: if admission controller returns “queued” vs “denied”, request might sit, then client times out and retries. We might end up executing both unless we cancel old one. That’s not just billing; that’s correctness.

Cole Summers: For v1 we’re doing “immediate admit/deny” (no queue) per ADR draft, right? So less risk. But we still have internal retries (data plane transient errors) that can re-run dispatch.

Ethan Park: Correct: immediate admit/deny for GA, no customer-visible queue semantics. Internal backpressure still exists but request either goes through baseline or burst or gets 429.

Nadia Rahman: Then we can scope idempotency to: same customer request retried at gateway layer, or data plane “attempts” for the same request.

Logan Wright: Data plane can generate an “execution_id” per attempt. If we have `billing_id` stable, we can guarantee only one execution contributes to billing by taking the first successful execution.

Cole Summers: Yup. Something like:
- stable `billing_id` at edge
- `attempt` increments per internal retry
- emit usage event only for `terminal_state=success|partial_success` with GPU-ms measured
- if multiple success events show up (bug), billing sink dedupes by (tenant_id, billing_id)

Allison Grant: Define what “partial_success” means for streaming. If we stream tokens and then disconnect, GPU time was still consumed. Do we bill that?

Rishi Malhotra: We generally bill for work performed. If we generated 2k tokens and customer disconnected, that’s still cost. So yes, bill what’s actually generated.

Cole Summers: That means metering should be based on server-side token counts + GPU-ms, not client receipt.

Laura Bennett: Contract language needs to be precise: “usage based on Redwood-measured compute/time and tokens generated” etc. Michael/Sofia are drafting but we should sync them on these semantics.

Jordan Lee: Another edge: denied burst but served on baseline. That should not create burst usage. But we might still emit a “denial” event for observability.

Nadia Rahman: Denial events should NOT go into billing sink unless we explicitly separate streams. Otherwise someone will accidentally sum admitted=false events.

Cole Summers: Good call. We can split:
- `BurstUsageEvent` (billable) emitted only when burst pool resources consumed
- `BurstDecisionEvent` (non-billable) for analytics/ops

Logan Wright: Please also keep schema versioned. If you change field names, downstream breaks. Add `schema_version` string.

Ethan Park: What about reconciliation with Finance reporting? Rishi asked daily revenue totals.

Nadia Rahman: We can provide a daily rollup job per tenant:
- sum(burst_gpu_ms) by tenant/day/region/hw_class
- compare to invoice line items produced by billing service
- alert if delta > threshold (e.g. 0.5% or $50)
But again, this only works if upstream events are stable/idempotent.

Cole Summers: Can we get a “replay window” expectation? If we detect a bug, can we re-export from raw logs?

Nadia Rahman: Yes, but it’s painful. If we store raw usage events in the data lake (S3) with partition by day, we can reprocess. I’d rather not rely on that for GA correctness.

Benji Okafor: Action items I see:
1) Add `x-redwood-billing-id` generation at edge + propagate through routing/data plane.
2) Update metering emitter to use billing-id for event_id.
3) Split decision vs usage events.

Allison Grant: + add a short doc in the runbook: “customer disputes burst charges” -> where to pull per-request breakdown (console export or internal debug endpoint).

Cole Summers: I’ll write up a quick design note + update ENG-4827 w/ these semantics. Also going to add a unit test: simulate retry path (same billing-id, two attempts) => exactly 1 billing record.

Rishi Malhotra: Please tag me once you have the definition in writing. Finance sign-off depends on “metering unit” + reconciliation story.

BillingPipelineBot: Reminder: billing-data-prod nightly validation runs at 02:00 UTC. Current dedupe key: `event_id`. If `event_id` is empty or not unique, duplicates will be counted.

Laura Bennett: Thanks all. Once we have the stable billing-id approach, I’m comfortable telling Legal “we can defend the measurement.”

Cole Summers: Cool. I’ll loop in Michael/Sofia separately. Closing loop here after I push the header + dedupe changes.

title: #architecture: 1739566123
preview:
Ravi Menon: Kicking off review for ADR-027 (throughput SLO metric definition). Need sign-off from SRE/Obs/Infra/Product before we lock it + route ENG work. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-027-throughput-slo-metric-definition

Ravi Menon: TL;DR proposal: canonical metric = tokens/sec at the deployment boundary, attributed per `deployment_id` (Dedicated), evaluated over sliding window (not per-request). Requests/sec stays as secondary/supporting metric.

Mina Haddad: +1 to per-deployment. Routing needs an unambiguous unit to protect, otherwise tier-aware admission control becomes guessy. One thing: we should call out whether tokens include prompt+completion (total) or completion only.

Sean Gallagher: Read it. Main concerns:
1) streaming accounting (we already saw double-counting in early metrics),
2) batching/caching: tokens/sec can look “too good” if cache hits aren’t separated,
3) multi-model deployments (same deployment_id w/ 2 models) -> attribution gets messy.

Allison Grant: For instrumentation, we should be explicit on labels/aggregation: `deployment_id`, `model`, `model_version`, `region`, `route`.
If the ADR says “per deployment”, we still need `model` dimension to debug + capacity plan.

Rafael Mendes: For alerting, I want the ADR to say what we page on:
- SLO signal = min(throughput floor compliance over 5m) AND sustained over 30m for incident
- burn-rate windows rely on consistent denominator
If the metric definition is ambiguous, burn-rate templates will be noisy.

Irene Choi: Infra POV: tokens/sec is fine but ONLY if we constrain the measurement conditions. Shape variance across A100/H100/L40S is big, esp long context. We need ADR language that the SLO metric is evaluated against a pinned model+version+max_ctx and “supported config” list.

Quentin Harris: +1. Please add a sentence: if customer changes model, quantization profile, or context beyond supported limits, the throughput guarantee (and metric target) needs re-baselining.
Also: clarify whether “tokens” are post-tokenization model tokens (not characters).

Ethan Park: Control plane needs a clear “source of truth” for which config is “the deployment.” Today customers can change `max_batch_size` + `max_concurrency` without reprovisioning. Do we treat those as customer-tunable knobs that can invalidate the SLO, or do we lock them per tier?

Lauren Bishop: From product side, ADR shouldn’t read like contract language, but it should align with the PRD so we don’t end up promising “completion tokens/sec” in one place and “total tokens/sec” in another.
My vote: define canonical throughput as total tokens/sec (prompt+completion) at the API boundary, because customers care about end-to-end cost + time.

Sergio Costa: Dashboarding note: if we pick total tokens/sec, we need panels for prompt vs completion split anyway. Otherwise we’ll get escalations where a customer says “my output is slow” but their prompts got longer and total tokens/sec looks fine.

Sean Gallagher: Re: total vs completion-only: SRE preference is total tokens/sec for simplicity + fewer edge cases. But we must specify the counting point:
- count prompt tokens once per request at request accept
- count completion tokens as generated (streaming chunks)
- exclude retransmits/retries where client disconnects? (or at least define)

Mina Haddad: On disconnects: routing/admission control sees the request as accepted. If client drops mid-stream, we probably should count tokens actually produced, but not “expected” tokens. Otherwise you can get weird incentives.

Allison Grant: We can implement “generated_tokens_total” at server-side and “request_prompt_tokens” at accept. For disconnect, generated tokens stops. That’s clean.
Also please add: metric is computed from server-side counters, not client reported.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also add guidance that we de-dupe on `request_id` for retries where we can (server retry) and otherwise rely on idempotency keys.

Irene Choi: There’s also warmup/warm pool. First N minutes after deploy / scale-up is not representative. ADR should mention whether we exclude warmup time from SLO computations or whether the SLO target assumes warm pool exists for Plus/Premium.

Lauren Bishop: Warm pool policy is tiered (Premium yes, Standard not guaranteed). Let’s keep warmup exclusions in the SLO definition doc, but ADR can note that “measurement assumes deployment is in steady state and serving,” and point to where warmup is handled.

Sofia Mendes: Legal hat: agree ADR shouldn’t be contract text, but please avoid wording like “guarantee” in ADR. Use “SLO target” / “measurement signal.” If you add exclusions, be careful to phrase as “measurement scope” not “we don’t care.”

Kira Thompson: I can help edit for clarity. If we’re aligning docs, can we add a short glossary section:
- token (model token)
- prompt token vs completion token
- throughput signal vs SLO target
- deployment boundary

Quentin Harris: Also call out that tokenizer depends on model family. Metric uses the model’s tokenizer (or Redwood standard for that model). Otherwise “tokens/sec” is meaningless across models.

Ethan Park: For the “deployment boundary”: is it at the public API ingress or at the model worker? If at worker, routing retries/fallback can skew. If at ingress, we include queueing + admission effects.

Sean Gallagher: For SLO measurement, I think we want at the service boundary where customer sees performance (ingress). But the *throughput* is token generation, so it’s mostly worker-side. Proposed compromise:
- count tokens at worker generation (source of truth)
- attribute to deployment_id at ingress decision
That way fallback still counts against the deployment that accepted it (unless we explicitly define fallback semantics).

Mina Haddad: If we do that, ADR must specify fallback attribution. For Dedicated, fallback to a different pool/region should still count against the same deployment_id (customer’s Dedicated deployment) for SLO reporting, otherwise we “fix” misses by shifting traffic.

Rafael Mendes: +1, otherwise SLO dashboards lie and incident review is painful.

Sergio Costa: Noted. For dashboards, we can show both:
- “logical deployment throughput” (counts fallback)
- “physical pool throughput” (where it actually ran)
Helps debugging.

Ravi Menon: This is great. Action items I’m hearing for ADR text:
1) Define token counting (prompt once at accept, completion as generated; total tokens/sec canonical)
2) Clarify attribution: logical `deployment_id` at ingress decision; fallback counts toward same deployment_id; add optional “physical execution” dimension
3) Call out required labels: deployment_id/model/model_version/region/route
4) Note steady-state assumption + link out to warmup handling (tier-dependent)
5) Add glossary + avoid “guarantee” language

Ravi Menon: I’ll push edits today + tag for final sign-off. Target: EOD Thu.

ADR Bot: Ravi Menon updated “ADR-027-throughput-slo-metric-definition” (v7) in Confluence. Review requested from @Sean Gallagher, @Allison Grant, @Irene Choi, @Lauren Bishop, @Sofia Mendes.

Sean Gallagher: Reviewed v7. Looks good. Only nit: add one line that cached tokens are still “tokens” but we’ll also emit cache hit rate separately; SLO metric doesn’t try to back out caching.

Allison Grant: v7 LGTM. Added a comment on label cardinality (route vs endpoint) to avoid blowing up metrics. We can use `deployment_route_class` instead of raw path.

Irene Choi: v7 LGTM from Infra. Thanks for adding the “supported config/model pinning” note.

Lauren Bishop: v7 good. Please just ensure PRD + docs PR use the same “total tokens/sec” phrasing.

Sofia Mendes: v7 ok from Legal. Appreciate the wording changes.

Ravi Menon: Awesome. I’ll mark ADR as Accepted once I incorporate Sean’s caching sentence + Allison’s route_class suggestion. Thanks all :thumbsup:

title: #eng-platform: 1739549012
preview:
Ethan Park: quick q before we lock the readiness APIs — who should be allowed to mutate node readiness state vs read-only? also need a plan for audit events when image channel/version changes for a pool.

Benji Okafor: +1. current proto has:
- `GET /v1/fleet/nodes/{node_id}/readiness`
- `POST /v1/fleet/nodes/{node_id}/events` (validation results)
- `PATCH /v1/fleet/nodes/{node_id}/readiness` (state transition)
Right now auth is just “internal service token”. needs tightening.

Jordan Lee: for state machine integrity: only ONE writer should be authoritative for `PATCH readiness`.
If multiple actors can set `Ready/Quarantined` directly, we’ll end up in weird transitions.

Ethan Park: agree. thinking:
- provisioning orchestrator is the only caller that can advance Allocated->Provisioning->Validating->Ready (and quarantine on policy)
- validation agent can only submit validation artifacts/results (not set state)
- internal UI + SRE tooling should be read-only

Fiona McCarthy: security POV: least privilege + auditable identity.
“internal token” without scopes isn’t going to fly once Private customers ask about change control.
We need explicit scopes/roles for read vs write.

Marcus Lin: +1. also make sure any write endpoint logs the actor identity + request id. we’ve had incidents where service A impersonated service B via shared token.

Hanae Suzuki: are we using the standard service-to-service authz middleware (OIDC svc tokens w/ claims) or rolling our own here?

Benji Okafor: control-plane uses the OIDC svc tokens already. we can enforce:
- `aud=redwood-control-plane`
- `svc=provisioning-orchestrator`
- `scopes=[fleet.readiness.write]`

Ethan Park: yeah, should reuse the same middleware. question: do we also allow Platform ops (humans) to mutate state for break-glass?

Harish Reddy: pls yes but behind break-glass. sometimes we need to move a node to Quarantined when automation is stuck and capacity ops is on fire.

Fiona McCarthy: break-glass is fine if:
1) it’s a separate role (no default)
2) MFA / elevated session
3) every action emits an audit event w/ reason

Jordan Lee: +1. but don’t allow “set arbitrary state”. allow a constrained operator action like `POST /v1/fleet/nodes/{id}:quarantine` and `POST ...:retryValidation`.
Avoid `PATCH state=Ready` from humans.

Benji Okafor: that’s cleaner. we can keep `PATCH readiness` private to orchestrator only.
Then add operator endpoints w/ explicit verbs.

Priya Natarajan: +1. keep state machine consistent. let humans trigger actions, not set final state.
Also we should make the API contract explicit for who can do what; otherwise internal console will get “temporary” write perms and never lose them.

Connor O'Brien: +1 from releases: image channel changes are basically releases. needs audit trail + we need to know who flipped candidate->stable and why.

Ethan Park: ok let’s talk audit events for image channel changes. where should this live?
We have a pool-level config: `image_channel` (candidate/stable) and `image_version`.

Benji Okafor: we can emit audit events on `PATCH /v1/fleet/pools/{pool_id}` when image fields change.
Event type: `fleet.pool.image_channel_changed` and `fleet.pool.image_version_changed`.

Marcus Lin: please include both old + new values in the audit event. and include correlation ids:
- `actor_type` (service|human)
- `actor_id` (svc name or user)
- `request_id`
- `change_ticket` (Linear/Jira)
- `commit_sha` if change originated from Terraform apply

Hanae Suzuki: also retention. audit logs need to go to the central audit sink (not just app logs). do we have an existing “audit-events” topic?

Benji Okafor: yep, `audit-events-prod` Kafka topic (same as KMS changes). we can emit a structured JSON event, and keep a copy pointer in DB for quick UI display.

Fiona McCarthy: careful on “copy in DB”. DB copy is fine but audit sink is source of truth. also make sure tamper-resistance: append-only.

Jordan Lee: question: if Terraform sets `image_channel=stable` but value is already stable, do we emit an event?

Connor O'Brien: for releases we usually *don’t* want no-op spam, but we *do* want an event when someone *attempts* a flip and it’s rejected.

Ethan Park: ok:
- emit audit event on successful change (old!=new)
- emit audit event on rejected change (403/validation fail) as `fleet.pool.image_change_rejected` w/ reason

Harish Reddy: +1. rejected reasons are super helpful for ops (“why didn’t it switch?”).

Benji Okafor: authz proposal:
READ:
- `fleet.readiness.read` for internal UI, SRE, support tooling
WRITE:
- `fleet.readiness.write` ONLY for `provisioning-orchestrator`
- `fleet.readiness.artifacts.write` for `node-validation-agent` to post validation results
OPS ACTIONS:
- `fleet.readiness.ops` for `:quarantine` / `:retryValidation`

Fiona McCarthy: looks good. add a guardrail that `node-validation-agent` can only write artifacts for its own node identity (if we have node identity) OR force it to go through orchestrator.

Benji Okafor: we don’t have strong node identity yet (no SPIFFE on workers today). safer: agent posts to orchestrator (in-cluster), orchestrator forwards to readiness service with its token.

Ethan Park: +1. keep readiness service perimeter small. agent -> orchestrator only.

Priya Natarajan: can we document this in the service doc + ADR? I don’t want to rediscover this when we onboard the next region.

Benji Okafor: yep. I’ll update service doc + add an “AuthZ” section w/ endpoint matrix.
Also will add audit events on pool patch (image fields) + include actor claims.

Redwood Deploy Bot: PR opened: redwood/pr-18503 "add audit events for image channel changes" (WIP)

Marcus Lin: please tag sec-eng for review on PR. also double-check we’re not logging secrets in audit event payload.

Hanae Suzuki: +1 and include the minimal set in payload. no full terraform plan dumps pls.

Ethan Park: action items:
- Benji: implement scoped authz + operator verb endpoints + audit event emission
- Fiona/Marcus: review event schema + retention requirements
- Connor: define required fields for change mgmt (ticket id, release train) so it shows up in rollout checklist

Benji Okafor: ack. will post the endpoint matrix here once I’ve got it in docs.

Harish Reddy: thanks. ops will be happy if we can quarantine without asking someone to temporarily grant admin on the readiness service :)

Priya Natarajan: ship it, but keep the blast radius tight. no broad write scopes for console.

“internal token” without scopes isn’t going to fly once Private customers ask about change control.
We need explicit scopes/roles for read vs write.

Marcus Lin: +1. also make sure any write endpoint logs the actor identity + request id. we’ve had incidents where service A impersonated service B via shared token.

Hanae Suzuki: are we using the standard service-to-service authz middleware (OIDC svc tokens w/ claims) or rolling our own here?

Benji Okafor: control-plane uses the OIDC svc tokens already. we can enforce:
- `aud=redwood-control-plane`
- `svc=provisioning-orchestrator`
- `scopes=[fleet.readiness.write]`

Ethan Park: yeah, should reuse the same middleware. question: do we also allow Platform ops (humans) to mutate state for break-glass?

Harish Reddy: pls yes but behind break-glass. sometimes we need to move a node to Quarantined when automation is stuck and capacity ops is on fire.

Fiona McCarthy: break-glass is fine if:
1) it’s a separate role (no default)
2) MFA / elevated session
3) every action emits an audit event w/ reason

Jordan Lee: +1. but don’t allow “set arbitrary state”. allow a constrained operator action like `POST /v1/fleet/nodes/{id}:quarantine` and `POST ...:retryValidation`.
Avoid `PATCH state=Ready` from humans.

Benji Okafor: that’s cleaner. we can keep `PATCH readiness` private to orchestrator only.
Then add operator endpoints w/ explicit verbs.

Priya Natarajan: +1. keep state machine consistent. let humans trigger actions, not set final state.
Also we should make the API contract explicit for who can do what; otherwise internal console will get “temporary” write perms and never lose them.

Connor O'Brien: +1 from releases: image channel changes are basically releases. needs audit trail + we need to know who flipped candidate->stable and why.

Ethan Park: ok let’s talk audit events for image channel changes. where should this live?
We have a pool-level config: `image_channel` (candidate/stable) and `image_version`.

Benji Okafor: we can emit audit events on `PATCH /v1/fleet/pools/{pool_id}` when image fields change.
Event type: `fleet.pool.image_channel_changed` and `fleet.pool.image_version_changed`.

Marcus Lin: please include both old + new values in the audit event. and include correlation ids:
- `actor_type` (service|human)
- `actor_id` (svc name or user)
- `request_id`
- `change_ticket` (Linear/Jira)
- `commit_sha` if change originated from Terraform apply

Hanae Suzuki: also retention. audit logs need to go to the central audit sink (not just app logs). do we have an existing “audit-events” topic?

Benji Okafor: yep, `audit-events-prod` Kafka topic (same as KMS changes). we can emit a structured JSON event, and keep a copy pointer in DB for quick UI display.

Fiona McCarthy: careful on “copy in DB”. DB copy is fine but audit sink is source of truth. also make sure tamper-resistance: append-only.

Jordan Lee: question: if Terraform sets `image_channel=stable` but value is already stable, do we emit an event?

Connor O'Brien: for releases we usually *don’t* want no-op spam, but we *do* want an event when someone *attempts* a flip and it’s rejected.

Ethan Park: ok:
- emit audit event on successful change (old!=new)
- emit audit event on rejected change (403/validation fail) as `fleet.pool.image_change_rejected` w/ reason

Harish Reddy: +1. rejected reasons are super helpful for ops (“why didn’t it switch?”).

Benji Okafor: authz proposal:
READ:
- `fleet.readiness.read` for internal UI, SRE, support tooling
WRITE:
- `fleet.readiness.write` ONLY for `provisioning-orchestrator`
- `fleet.readiness.artifacts.write` for `node-validation-agent` to post validation results
OPS ACTIONS:
- `fleet.readiness.ops` for `:quarantine` / `:retryValidation`

Fiona McCarthy: looks good. add a guardrail that `node-validation-agent` can only write artifacts for its own node identity (if we have node identity) OR force it to go through orchestrator.

Benji Okafor: we don’t have strong node identity yet (no SPIFFE on workers today). safer: agent posts to orchestrator (in-cluster), orchestrator forwards to readiness service with its token.

Ethan Park: +1. keep readiness service perimeter small. agent -> orchestrator only.

Priya Natarajan: can we document this in the service doc + ADR? I don’t want to rediscover this when we onboard the next region.

Benji Okafor: yep. I’ll update service doc + add an “AuthZ” section w/ endpoint matrix.
Also will add audit events on pool patch (image fields) + include actor claims.

Redwood Deploy Bot: PR opened: redwood/pr-18503 "add audit events for image channel changes" (WIP)

Marcus Lin: please tag sec-eng for review on PR. also double-check we’re not logging secrets in audit event payload.

Hanae Suzuki: +1 and include the minimal set in payload. no full terraform plan dumps pls.

Ethan Park: action items:
- Benji: implement scoped authz + operator verb endpoints + audit event emission
- Fiona/Marcus: review event schema + retention requirements
- Connor: define required fields for change mgmt (ticket id, release train) so it shows up in rollout checklist

Benji Okafor: ack. will post the endpoint matrix here once I’ve got it in docs.

Harish Reddy: thanks. ops will be happy if we can quarantine without asking someone to temporarily grant admin on the readiness service :)

Priya Natarajan: ship it, but keep the blast radius tight. no broad write scopes for console.

title: #architecture: 1739520203
preview:
Ethan Park: need final eyes on ADR before we mark “ready for approval” today. main open item: make the decision points super explicit + document rejected alternatives (blue/green etc). anyone have last-minute concerns?

Talia Benson: +1 please. we keep getting the same questions in review: “why not blue/green” and “what exactly is rollback guaranteed to do.”

Connor O'Brien: read through the draft this morning. i think we need a crisp “Decision” section w/ bullets:
- in-place upgrade driven by generated upgrade plan artifact
- N-1 -> N only initially
- rollback is best-effort but deterministic within contract (and what’s out of scope)

Sean Gallagher: biggest thing for me is we don’t oversell rollback. should say: rollback is guaranteed to restore control-plane + config state to prior version *if* prechecks pass + backup completed + we haven’t crossed non-reversible migration boundary.

Michael Nguyen: on “non-reversible”: we should be very direct about schema migrations.
- default is backward-compatible migrations (additive) for N-1 -> N
- if we ever need a breaking migration, rollback becomes “restore-from-backup required” and we treat as manual / escalated path

Benji Okafor: +1. also include that we’re not relying on `helm rollback` as the mechanism. helm rollback alone doesn’t revert CRD changes / externalized state.

Rafael Mendes: yep. oncall perspective: we need a simple “rollback entry points” paragraph:
- rollback before CP cutover (safe)
- rollback after CP upgraded but before DP rollout (should be safe)
- rollback after DP changes (depends on migrations + workload)

Ethan Park: makes sense. ADR currently has “phases” but not the explicit contract language.

Quentin Harris: can we add the compatibility policy tie-in? i.e. upgrade plan generation is gated by policy evaluation, blocks unsupported jumps.

Connor O'Brien: yes please. reviewers keep asking “what stops someone from jumping 2 major versions.”

Naomi Feldman: from security side: rejected alternatives should mention why blue/green isn’t automatically “safer” for regulated on-prem. data duplication + two live control planes + key material handling. also cost.

Marcus Lin: also network: blue/green implies DNS / LB cutovers. in private deployments we can’t assume customer has automated traffic shifting, esp on-prem with static VIPs.

Allison Grant: observability note: in-place with plan gives us an auditable step log and we can emit structured events per step. blue/green cutover tends to look like a single flip unless we build extra instrumentation.

Sean Gallagher: also operationally, blue/green doubles failure modes (two clusters to keep healthy, drift, split-brain if control plane talks to wrong dp). i’d love that called out explicitly.

Talia Benson: ok but we should be fair: blue/green *does* reduce blast radius sometimes. so ADR should say “considered, rejected for v1 due to X” not “never.”

Ethan Park: agreed. section: “Rejected alternatives (for v1)” and include a “revisit when we have…” list.

Michael Nguyen: wording suggestion for migrations:
- “We optimize for forward-only additive migrations to preserve rollbackability via versioned deploy rollback + config restore.”
- “When not possible, rollback requires restore-from-backup; installer will surface ‘manual intervention required’ and stop.”

Rafael Mendes: +1 add explicit “stop points.” oncall needs clear criteria: when rollback is recommended vs when to escalate.

Connor O'Brien: also call out why “always rebuild cluster” isn’t viable: on-prem hardware procurement / scheduling + long drain times. plus customers don’t want new cluster IDs/certs every upgrade.

Benji Okafor: don’t forget “blue/green inside same cluster via namespaces” option. we looked at it: still needs CRD versioning, shared DB, shared LB => not actually isolated.

Ethan Park: yep. list of alternatives to capture:
1) full blue/green cluster swap
2) namespace-based blue/green
3) helm-only rollback
4) immutable “recreate everything” (tear down + reinstall)

Sean Gallagher: and for each, one paragraph on why it’s rejected. keep it short.

Quentin Harris: for #1, add: requires duplicate capacity (GPUs esp) + artifact mirrors for both stacks. for VPC customers maybe ok, for on-prem often impossible.

Naomi Feldman: for #3 helm-only rollback: add security angle: doesn’t roll back secrets refs / external KMS config changes, so could leave cluster in inconsistent auth state.

Marcus Lin: for blue/green: mention customer networking constraints: changing endpoint IPs / internal DNS propagation can take minutes-hours. can’t do safe cutover inside the upgrade SLO.

Allison Grant: “decision point” i want in ADR: we store upgrade plan + execution state as an auditable artifact (support bundle + audit log events). that’s a key differentiator vs ad-hoc scripts.

Connor O'Brien: +1 and tie to change-management gating: prechecks must pass, backups must succeed before any irreversible step.

Ethan Park: ok action items i’ll do now:
- add explicit “Decision” bullets
- add “Rollback contract” subsection (guarantees + stop points)
- add “Rejected alternatives (v1)” section w/ blue/green + others
- add “Revisit” criteria (ex: when we have traffic shifting primitives + dual-write migrations)

Sean Gallagher: can you also add a sentence: “rollback is a first-class command that consumes stored state; it is idempotent” ? reviewers keep asking.

Ethan Park: yes.

Talia Benson: timeline-wise, can we get the updated doc in ~1 hr so i can attach to the approval request?

Ethan Park: yep. will post link when updated.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: also please add a note that N-1 -> N is enforced by policy in the installer, not just “recommended.”

Quentin Harris: :thumbsup: i can review the policy wording once you paste.

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: ping me on the security bits.

Ethan Park: updated ADR draft is up w/ new sections. please review: rejected alternatives now includes blue/green + namespace-based + helm-only + recreate. link in drive: https://confluence.redwoodinference.local/x/ADR-Private-Upgrade-Plan-Rollback (same page)

Sean Gallagher: reading now. first pass looks good. “stop points” paragraph is what we needed.

Connor O'Brien: skimmed. +1. small nit: in “Revisit blue/green” add “when we can guarantee deterministic CP<->DP pairing during cutover” (avoid split brain).

Ethan Park: added.

Allison Grant: love the auditability callout. also added note re: emitting audit events per phase.

Talia Benson: looks good to me. moving to approval request w/ SRE + Release Eng.

Ethan Park: thanks all. marking ADR “final review complete” unless more comments come in.

title: #architecture: 1739450020
preview:
Talia Benson: Kicking off a thread to coordinate the architecture review for route-level quantization enablement + fallback behavior. Need sign-off this week to keep the schema/API/routing work unblocked.

Talia Benson: ADR draft is here (WIP): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-0137-Route-level-quantization-profile-enablement

Talia Benson: Proposed review slot: Thu 11:00–11:45 PT. Attendees: Platform (Priya/Ethan/Mina/Ravi/Jordan), Runtime (Mateo/Noah), Console (Dylan/Chloe), Security/RBAC (Naomi/Marcus), PM (Marissa). If you can’t make it, pls comment on the ADR.

Marissa Cole: +1. Main thing from Product side: must be explicitly opt-in per route for v1, no global “tenant default enables everything.” Also needs “easy rollback” story that Support can execute.

Priya Natarajan: Thanks Talia. Read through ADR quickly. Overall direction seems right. I want to make sure we’re crisp on:
- what exactly is stored in route config (profile_id vs profile_ref vs alias)
- where compatibility is enforced (policy engine vs runtime)
- deterministic resolution (same request config => same profile)

Ethan Park: I can make Thu.
Question: ADR says “route references a profile ref string” and examples show both `recommended` and a versioned id. We should avoid anything that can change under customer feet.

Mina Haddad: +1. If we allow `recommended` alias, then “recommended” moving to a new version changes behavior without an explicit config write. That’s basically hidden rollout.

Ravi Menon: Agree. My suggestion: v1 should only allow explicit versioned `quant_profile_id` (immutable). If we need an alias later, treat it as a separate field with “pinning” semantics.

Jordan Lee: Determinism hat on: any alias that’s resolved at request time based on “latest” breaks determinism unless we snapshot it into resolved policy at write time.

Noah Patel: Runtime side: we can handle either, but if policy engine resolves to an explicit immutable id before the request hits the runtime, it’s cleaner. Runtime should never call “what’s the newest profile” mid-request.

Mateo Alvarez: Agree w/ Noah. Runtime should consume resolved result: {enabled, profile_id, reason_if_fallback}. Everything else belongs in control plane.

Dylan Brooks: Console UX question: customers will ask “just use recommended.” But we can still show “recommended” in UI and when they click enable, we write the pinned versioned ID. That gives UX simplicity without the hidden change risk.

Chloe Martin: That’s workable for backend too. Console can call a “preview” endpoint that returns which profile id would be pinned + compatibility warnings.

Naomi Feldman: For audit/compliance: we need the audit event to include the exact pinned profile id and diff. “Enabled recommended” is not sufficient for change control.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also need clarity on who can enable/disable this. Ideally a separate permission (route config write but with elevated capability). At minimum, record actor + route + old/new profile.

Talia Benson: Sounds like we’re converging on: UI may say “recommended” but route config stores immutable `quant_profile_id` (or `default`). I’ll add that as a decision point in ADR.

Priya Natarajan: On compatibility enforcement: ADR currently says policy engine checks model/hardware/seq length and falls back to baseline. Runtime also has guardrails.
We should decide: which layer is authoritative? (I’d like policy engine to be authoritative; runtime should still validate to protect itself.)

Jordan Lee: Agree. Policy eval should produce a single resolved decision, including fallback, to avoid oscillation.
Also: if runtime independently decides to fallback, we must avoid a feedback loop where policy keeps trying and runtime keeps rejecting.

Ravi Menon: +1 on “no oscillation.” I propose we include a `resolution_outcome` in the resolved policy: `APPLIED | FALLBACK_INCOMPATIBLE | DISABLED | KILL_SWITCH`.
Runtime logs it but shouldn’t re-resolve.

Noah Patel: Runtime still has to protect against mismatched artifacts / corrupted profile / unsupported kernel path. In those cases we should fail closed to baseline and emit metrics.
But we should NOT attempt to re-query policy. Just do local fallback.

Mateo Alvarez: Exactly. Runtime local fallback is last resort; also needs a counter so SRE can alert when it happens.

Connor O'Brien: From release/oncall POV: we need a clear kill switch behavior in the ADR:
- global kill switch disables application everywhere (even if route is enabled)
- should be fast propagating (minutes)
- should still keep system healthy (fallback baseline)

Mina Haddad: Routing view: if kill switch is on, route selection should still pick the same model variant; only precision/quant mode changes.
Do not redirect to different region/tier unless we’re already in a failure mode.

Ethan Park: Another detail: caching.
- route config service caching resolved settings is good
- but we must ensure config updates invalidate cache quickly
- and resolved decision is included in request context so runtime doesn’t do extra calls

Chloe Martin: +1. Our control plane endpoint can return “resolved quant settings” with a TTL + etag. Router can cache on route_id + model_variant + region.

Priya Natarajan: Fallback semantics: ADR says “fallback to baseline precision.” Need to define baseline precisely (e.g., model’s default serving mode). Also define what happens if baseline also not available (separate normal routing fallback).

Ravi Menon: Suggest: quantization fallback is orthogonal. If quant profile incompatible/unavailable -> serve with route’s normal model variant at baseline precision. If the model itself is unavailable -> normal routing fallback logic (existing).

Jordan Lee: We should explicitly state order of operations in ADR:
1) route -> model variant selection (existing)
2) resolve quant setting (enabled? which profile?)
3) compatibility check using selected model variant + hardware class + max_context
4) if incompatible -> set resolution outcome to fallback baseline

Noah Patel: +1. Also sequence length constraints: profiles might be validated only up to N ctx. If request ctx > N, we should fallback rather than attempting to run and risking correctness/perf.

Mateo Alvarez: That should be in compatibility matrix (profile declares `max_context_tokens` or similar). Runtime has the true request ctx at execution time; if request exceeds, runtime must fallback.

Marissa Cole: Product requirement: when fallback happens (incompatibility), customer should see it in Console + be able to alert on it. Otherwise they’ll think they’re saving money but they aren’t.

Dylan Brooks: Console can show “enabled but falling back X%” at route level. Need consistent signal name + reason.

Naomi Feldman: On audit: if kill switch overrides, that should also be a logged event with actor (SRE) and reason (incident ticket link maybe).

Connor O'Brien: We can do that. Also would like a “runbook” link in ADR so we’re aligned. I’ll drop the existing rollback doc once it’s merged.

Talia Benson: Action items before review:
- Priya/Jordan/Ravi: propose final decision on alias vs pinned profile id (sounds pinned)
- Noah/Mateo: propose runtime last-resort fallback behavior + metrics
- Naomi/Marcus: confirm RBAC/audit requirements (who can toggle, what gets logged)
- Dylan/Chloe: confirm Console flow: preview -> write pinned id -> show warnings

Priya Natarajan: I’ll add comments in ADR today.

Jordan Lee: I’ll comment with determinism language + avoid alias resolution at request time.

Noah Patel: I’ll add a section to ADR for runtime responsibilities vs policy responsibilities, and the “no re-query” rule.

Marcus Lin: I’ll add security notes. One ask: ensure profile artifacts are integrity checked (signing / checksum) and failures trigger fallback.

Talia Benson: Great. See you Thu unless conflicts. :thumbsup:

(DeployBot): Reminder: Architecture Review "Quantization Profile Enablement + Fallbacks" scheduled Thu 11:00 PT (Zoom link in calendar invite).
